items.text: IT costs are systematically tracked and categorized across all services and business units.

rubric.initial: IT costs are not tracked at a granular level; spending is aggregated with no visibility into cost drivers.
rubric.basic: Some cost tracking exists but categories are inconsistent and data is often outdated.
rubric.intermediate: IT costs are categorized using a standardized taxonomy and tracked regularly with documented processes.
rubric.advanced: Real-time cost tracking is in place with automated data collection, full transparency, and drill-down capabilities.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive cost analytics drive proactive budget decisions, with continuous benchmarking against industry standards.

items.text: Cost allocation models accurately reflect IT resource consumption by business units.

rubric.initial: No cost allocation model exists; IT is treated as a flat overhead expense.
rubric.basic: A rudimentary allocation model exists but uses simplistic metrics like headcount rather than actual consumption.
rubric.intermediate: Allocation models use documented activity-based or usage-based metrics reviewed on a regular schedule.
rubric.advanced: Allocation models are integrated with usage telemetry, providing accurate consumption-based costing per service.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic allocation models self-adjust based on real-time consumption data and predictive demand patterns.

items.text: Financial reports for IT spending are accessible to relevant stakeholders and support decision-making.

rubric.initial: Financial reports are rarely produced and are not shared with business stakeholders.
rubric.basic: Periodic reports are generated but lack detail and are not tailored to stakeholder needs.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized financial reports are produced regularly and distributed to all relevant stakeholders.
rubric.advanced: Interactive dashboards provide stakeholders with self-service access to IT financial data with drill-down capabilities.
rubric.optimizing: AI-augmented financial insights are delivered proactively, highlighting anomalies, trends, and optimization opportunities.

items.text: The organization uses chargeback or showback mechanisms to communicate IT costs to business units.

rubric.initial: No chargeback or showback mechanism exists; business units have no visibility into their IT consumption costs.
rubric.basic: A basic showback report is produced but is infrequent and not tied to actual service usage.
rubric.intermediate: Showback or chargeback processes are documented, standardized, and delivered on a regular cadence.
rubric.advanced: Chargeback models are fully integrated with service catalogs and financial systems, driving cost-conscious behavior.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic pricing models incentivize efficient resource usage and are continuously refined based on consumption patterns.

items.text: Business units can forecast their IT costs based on planned consumption of IT services.

rubric.initial: Business units have no ability to estimate or forecast their IT costs.
rubric.basic: Rough cost estimates are available but are based on historical averages rather than planned consumption.
rubric.intermediate: Cost forecasting tools are available with documented rate cards linked to the service catalog.
rubric.advanced: Self-service forecasting is integrated with demand management, enabling accurate budget planning by business units.
rubric.optimizing: Machine-learning models provide predictive cost forecasts that account for seasonal patterns, growth trends, and planned initiatives.

items.text: IT financial data is integrated with enterprise financial systems for consistent reporting.

rubric.initial: IT financial data exists in isolated spreadsheets with no integration to enterprise systems.
rubric.basic: Some data feeds exist between IT and enterprise finance but reconciliation is manual.
rubric.intermediate: Automated integration ensures IT financial data flows into enterprise systems with regular reconciliation.
rubric.advanced: Real-time bidirectional integration provides a single source of truth for IT and enterprise financial data.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent data integration automatically resolves discrepancies and enriches financial data with operational context.

items.text: IT planning processes are formally linked to business planning cycles and strategic objectives.

rubric.initial: IT planning is disconnected from business planning; IT responds reactively to business requests.
rubric.basic: Some informal coordination exists between IT and business planning, but timelines and outputs are misaligned.
rubric.intermediate: IT planning is formally synchronized with business planning cycles with documented input and output touchpoints.
rubric.advanced: Integrated planning processes ensure IT initiatives are prioritized based on measurable business value.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous, adaptive planning enables IT and business to co-create strategies with real-time feedback loops.

items.text: Business stakeholders actively participate in IT planning and prioritization processes.

rubric.initial: Business stakeholders are not involved in IT planning; IT sets its own priorities.
rubric.basic: Business input is sought occasionally but not through a structured or repeatable process.
rubric.intermediate: Defined roles and forums ensure regular business stakeholder participation in IT planning.
rubric.advanced: Cross-functional planning teams with clear accountability drive collaborative IT-business prioritization.
rubric.optimizing: Business and IT leaders jointly own the planning process with shared KPIs and mutual accountability for outcomes.

items.text: IT business plans include measurable objectives, milestones, and success criteria.

rubric.initial: IT plans lack measurable objectives; success is not formally defined or tracked.
rubric.basic: Some plans include objectives but they are vague and lack clear milestones or success criteria.
rubric.intermediate: All major IT plans include documented SMART objectives, milestones, and success criteria.
rubric.advanced: Plans are tracked against objectives with automated reporting and variance analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Objectives are dynamically adjusted based on real-time performance data and changing business conditions.

items.text: A multi-year IT strategic roadmap exists and is maintained with regular updates.

rubric.initial: No IT roadmap exists; planning is limited to immediate operational needs.
rubric.basic: A roadmap exists but is outdated, incomplete, or not reviewed regularly.
rubric.intermediate: A multi-year roadmap is maintained, reviewed quarterly, and aligned with business strategy.
rubric.advanced: The roadmap integrates dependencies, resource constraints, and business value projections with scenario planning.
rubric.optimizing: The roadmap is a living artifact continuously updated through automated feeds from portfolio, demand, and market intelligence.

items.text: IT planning cycles produce actionable outputs that translate into funded projects and initiatives.

rubric.initial: Planning outputs are abstract and rarely translate into concrete, funded initiatives.
rubric.basic: Some plans lead to funded projects but the linkage between planning and execution is weak.
rubric.intermediate: Planning cycles consistently produce prioritized initiative lists with approved budgets and timelines.
rubric.advanced: Planning outputs are directly integrated with portfolio management and financial approval workflows.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous planning enables rapid reallocation of funding based on real-time value realization data.

items.text: Business planning incorporates technology trend analysis to anticipate disruption and opportunity.

rubric.initial: Technology trends are not considered in business planning processes.
rubric.basic: Technology trends are occasionally mentioned but not systematically analyzed for business impact.
rubric.intermediate: Structured technology trend analysis is a standard input to business planning with documented implications.
rubric.advanced: Scenario planning integrates technology trends with business strategy to identify disruption risks and opportunities.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous technology intelligence feeds automatically update business plans with emerging trend impacts.

items.text: Key IT-enabled business processes are documented with clearly defined inputs, outputs, and owners.

rubric.initial: Processes are undocumented and exist only as tribal knowledge.
rubric.basic: Some processes are documented but documentation is incomplete, inconsistent, or outdated.
rubric.intermediate: All key processes are documented in a standard format with defined owners and regular review cycles.
rubric.advanced: Process documentation is integrated with workflow tools, enabling real-time monitoring and compliance tracking.
rubric.optimizing: Process models are auto-generated from system telemetry and continuously validated against actual execution patterns.

items.text: Process performance is measured using defined KPIs and benchmarks.

rubric.initial: No process performance metrics exist; there is no visibility into process efficiency.
rubric.basic: Basic metrics are collected for some processes but are not consistently tracked or acted upon.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized KPIs are defined for all key processes and reviewed on a regular schedule.
rubric.advanced: Process KPIs are monitored in real time with automated alerting for deviations and bottlenecks.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive analytics identify process degradation before it impacts outcomes, enabling preemptive optimization.

items.text: The organization systematically identifies and implements process improvement opportunities.

rubric.initial: Process improvements happen only in response to failures or complaints.
rubric.basic: Improvement efforts occur sporadically without a systematic approach or methodology.
rubric.intermediate: A formal process improvement methodology is in place with regular review cycles and prioritized improvement backlogs.
rubric.advanced: Process mining and analytics drive data-informed improvement initiatives with measurable ROI tracking.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous process optimization is embedded in operations, with AI-driven recommendations and autonomous improvements.

items.text: Automation is applied strategically to reduce manual effort and improve process consistency.

rubric.initial: Processes are predominantly manual with no automation strategy.
rubric.basic: Some ad hoc automation exists but is not governed or strategically planned.
rubric.intermediate: An automation roadmap prioritizes high-value opportunities with standardized tools and governance.
rubric.advanced: Intelligent automation (RPA, workflow engines) is broadly deployed with measurable efficiency gains.
rubric.optimizing: Hyperautomation integrates AI, RPA, and low-code platforms to continuously discover and automate end-to-end processes.

items.text: Process performance metrics are linked to business outcomes to demonstrate IT-enabled value.

rubric.initial: Process performance metrics are not collected or linked to business outcomes.
rubric.basic: Some process metrics exist but linkage to business outcomes is informal.
rubric.intermediate: Key process metrics are mapped to business outcomes with regular reporting on IT-enabled improvements.
rubric.advanced: Integrated dashboards show real-time process performance impact on business KPIs.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive process analytics forecast business outcome improvements from proposed process changes.

items.text: IT demand is forecasted using data-driven methods that account for business growth and seasonal patterns.

rubric.initial: No demand forecasting is performed; capacity decisions are purely reactive.
rubric.basic: Demand is estimated using informal methods or historical averages with limited accuracy.
rubric.intermediate: Documented forecasting methods use historical data and business input on a regular schedule.
rubric.advanced: Statistical models incorporate multiple variables including business plans, usage trends, and external factors.
rubric.optimizing: Machine learning models provide continuous, highly accurate demand forecasts with confidence intervals and scenario analysis.

items.text: Capacity forecasts are shared with stakeholders and integrated into budgeting and procurement processes.

rubric.initial: Capacity forecasts are not produced or shared with stakeholders.
rubric.basic: Forecasts are shared informally but are not integrated into financial or procurement planning.
rubric.intermediate: Forecasts are formally communicated to stakeholders and feed into annual budget and procurement cycles.
rubric.advanced: Forecasts are integrated with financial planning systems, enabling automated procurement triggers and budget adjustments.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time capacity intelligence drives dynamic resource provisioning and continuous financial optimization.

items.text: Infrastructure and platform capacity is monitored and planned to meet current and future demand.

rubric.initial: Capacity is not monitored; outages and performance issues are common due to insufficient resources.
rubric.basic: Basic monitoring exists but capacity planning is reactive, typically triggered by incidents.
rubric.intermediate: Capacity is regularly monitored against thresholds with documented planning for growth and peak periods.
rubric.advanced: Automated capacity management adjusts resources dynamically based on real-time demand and predefined policies.
rubric.optimizing: Self-healing infrastructure with predictive scaling ensures optimal capacity with minimal waste and zero service impact.

items.text: The organization has a scalability strategy that addresses both cloud and on-premises resource growth.

rubric.initial: No scalability strategy exists; growth is handled through emergency procurement.
rubric.basic: Some scalability considerations exist for cloud resources but on-premises planning remains ad hoc.
rubric.intermediate: A documented scalability strategy covers cloud and on-premises resources with defined scaling triggers.
rubric.advanced: Hybrid scalability policies are integrated with workload management and cost optimization frameworks.
rubric.optimizing: Autonomous scaling across hybrid environments optimizes cost, performance, and sustainability in real time.

items.text: Capacity models account for cloud elasticity and hybrid infrastructure scaling patterns.

rubric.initial: Capacity planning does not account for cloud resources or elastic scaling.
rubric.basic: Cloud capacity is managed separately from on-premises with no integrated view.
rubric.intermediate: Integrated capacity models cover hybrid environments with documented scaling policies.
rubric.advanced: Dynamic capacity management automatically adjusts across hybrid environments based on demand patterns.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven capacity orchestration optimizes placement and scaling across all infrastructure tiers.

items.text: A comprehensive service catalog defines available IT services with clear descriptions, SLAs, and pricing.

rubric.initial: No service catalog exists; users are unsure what IT services are available or how to request them.
rubric.basic: A basic catalog lists some services but lacks SLAs, pricing, or consistent descriptions.
rubric.intermediate: A standardized service catalog covers all major services with defined SLAs, pricing, and request procedures.
rubric.advanced: The service catalog is integrated with self-service portals, automated fulfillment, and real-time availability data.
rubric.optimizing: An intelligent service catalog uses AI to recommend services, predict demand, and dynamically adjust offerings.

items.text: IT demand is prioritized using a transparent, criteria-based process involving business stakeholders.

rubric.initial: Demand is addressed on a first-come, first-served basis with no formal prioritization.
rubric.basic: Informal prioritization occurs but criteria are unclear and decisions lack transparency.
rubric.intermediate: A documented prioritization framework uses defined criteria such as business value, urgency, and strategic alignment.
rubric.advanced: Demand prioritization is integrated with portfolio management, with real-time visibility into queues and trade-offs.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic prioritization uses real-time business impact analysis and predictive models to continuously optimize the demand pipeline.

items.text: Demand management processes balance short-term requests with long-term strategic initiatives.

rubric.initial: Short-term requests dominate; strategic initiatives are consistently deferred or underfunded.
rubric.basic: Awareness of the imbalance exists but no formal mechanism addresses the split between operational and strategic demand.
rubric.intermediate: Defined allocation policies reserve capacity for strategic work alongside operational demand.
rubric.advanced: Integrated demand management provides visibility into capacity allocation across run, grow, and transform categories.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive demand management dynamically rebalances investment across horizons based on value realization data.

items.text: IT supply capacity is aligned with demand through structured resource planning and allocation processes.

rubric.initial: Supply and demand are managed independently with frequent mismatches.
rubric.basic: Some coordination exists but resource allocation is largely reactive and manual.
rubric.intermediate: Resource planning processes formally match supply to demand with documented allocation rules.
rubric.advanced: Integrated demand-supply management optimizes resource utilization with scenario modeling and what-if analysis.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven supply optimization continuously balances internal capacity, cloud resources, and partner capabilities against demand.

items.text: The organization manages supply constraints proactively to minimize impact on service delivery.

rubric.initial: Supply constraints are discovered only when they cause service failures.
rubric.basic: Known constraints are tracked but mitigation is reactive rather than planned.
rubric.intermediate: Constraint management processes identify risks early and trigger documented mitigation actions.
rubric.advanced: Predictive analytics identify emerging constraints and automatically trigger pre-approved mitigation responses.
rubric.optimizing: Self-optimizing supply chains autonomously resolve constraints through multi-source orchestration and dynamic reallocation.

items.text: Demand management processes include mechanisms to shape demand toward strategic priorities.

rubric.initial: No demand shaping occurs; all requests are treated as equally valid.
rubric.basic: Some informal steering of demand exists but mechanisms are not systematic.
rubric.intermediate: Documented demand shaping mechanisms use pricing, service tiers, or prioritization to align demand with strategy.
rubric.advanced: Integrated demand management shapes consumption through incentives, self-service, and strategic communication.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive demand shaping dynamically adjusts mechanisms based on capacity, strategy, and value delivery signals.

items.text: A data governance framework defines policies, roles, and accountability for enterprise data management.

rubric.initial: No data governance exists; data ownership and accountability are undefined.
rubric.basic: Informal data governance practices exist in pockets but lack organization-wide policies or roles.
rubric.intermediate: A formal data governance framework is documented with defined data stewards, policies, and review processes.
rubric.advanced: Data governance is operationalized across the enterprise with automated policy enforcement and compliance monitoring.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive data governance uses AI to detect policy violations, recommend improvements, and evolve policies based on usage patterns.

items.text: Data quality is measured, monitored, and systematically improved across critical data domains.

rubric.initial: Data quality is unknown; issues are discovered only when they cause downstream errors.
rubric.basic: Ad hoc data quality checks are performed but there are no standardized metrics or monitoring.
rubric.intermediate: Data quality dimensions are defined and measured for critical domains with regular remediation cycles.
rubric.advanced: Automated data quality monitoring provides real-time scorecards with root cause analysis and remediation workflows.
rubric.optimizing: Self-healing data pipelines detect and correct quality issues autonomously, with continuous improvement of quality rules.

items.text: Master data is managed consistently across systems to ensure a single source of truth.

rubric.initial: No master data management exists; duplicate and conflicting data proliferates across systems.
rubric.basic: Master data is managed for some domains but synchronization across systems is incomplete.
rubric.intermediate: A master data management program covers key domains with defined golden records and synchronization processes.
rubric.advanced: Integrated MDM platforms ensure real-time consistency across all systems with automated conflict resolution.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent MDM uses graph-based matching and AI to maintain data integrity across complex, evolving ecosystems.

items.text: Information lifecycle policies govern the creation, retention, archival, and disposal of enterprise data.

rubric.initial: No lifecycle policies exist; data accumulates indefinitely with no management.
rubric.basic: Basic retention policies exist for some data types but enforcement is inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Comprehensive lifecycle policies are documented and enforced for all major data classifications.
rubric.advanced: Automated lifecycle management enforces policies across all repositories with audit trails and compliance reporting.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent lifecycle management uses content analysis and regulatory intelligence to dynamically optimize retention and disposal.

items.text: Data classification standards are applied to enable appropriate handling, access, and protection of information.

rubric.initial: No data classification scheme exists; all data is treated identically regardless of sensitivity.
rubric.basic: A classification scheme is defined but application is manual and inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Classification standards are enforced through documented processes and periodic audits across key systems.
rubric.advanced: Automated classification tools tag data at creation with policy-driven access controls and protection measures.
rubric.optimizing: AI-powered classification continuously evaluates and reclassifies data based on content analysis and evolving regulatory requirements.

items.text: Data lifecycle management policies ensure information is retained, archived, and disposed of appropriately.

rubric.initial: No data lifecycle management policies exist; data accumulates without governance.
rubric.basic: Some retention policies exist but are inconsistently applied and rarely enforced.
rubric.intermediate: Comprehensive lifecycle policies cover retention, archival, and disposal with regular compliance reviews.
rubric.advanced: Automated lifecycle management enforces policies with classification-based retention and disposition workflows.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent lifecycle management uses content analysis and regulatory monitoring to optimize data management.

items.text: IT energy consumption is measured and reported with targets for efficiency improvement.

rubric.initial: IT energy consumption is not measured or tracked.
rubric.basic: Basic energy metrics are collected for some facilities but not comprehensively or regularly.
rubric.intermediate: Energy consumption is measured across all major IT assets with documented reduction targets and regular reporting.
rubric.advanced: Real-time energy monitoring drives automated optimization with measurable progress against ambitious targets.
rubric.optimizing: AI-optimized energy management minimizes consumption while maintaining performance, contributing to organizational net-zero goals.

items.text: Sustainable IT practices are embedded in procurement, operations, and disposal processes.

rubric.initial: Sustainability is not considered in IT procurement or operations decisions.
rubric.basic: Some sustainability criteria exist for procurement but are not consistently applied across operations.
rubric.intermediate: Sustainability criteria are standardized in procurement, operations, and e-waste disposal with documented procedures.
rubric.advanced: Lifecycle sustainability assessments inform all technology decisions with supplier sustainability scorecards.
rubric.optimizing: Circular economy principles are fully embedded, with IT achieving measurable contributions to organizational sustainability goals.

items.text: The carbon footprint of IT operations is quantified and included in organizational sustainability reporting.

rubric.initial: IT carbon footprint is not measured or reported.
rubric.basic: Rough estimates of IT carbon impact exist but are based on incomplete data.
rubric.intermediate: IT carbon footprint is calculated using documented methodologies and reported in organizational sustainability reports.
rubric.advanced: Granular carbon accounting covers Scope 1, 2, and 3 IT emissions with verified reduction trajectories.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time carbon intelligence enables carbon-aware workload placement and continuous emissions optimization across the IT estate.

items.text: Environmental impact metrics are used to inform technology selection and architecture decisions.

rubric.initial: Environmental impact is not a factor in technology decisions.
rubric.basic: Environmental considerations are occasionally raised but do not systematically influence decisions.
rubric.intermediate: Environmental impact criteria are included in technology evaluation frameworks and architecture review processes.
rubric.advanced: Environmental impact is weighted alongside cost, performance, and risk in all major technology decisions.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive environmental modeling enables proactive selection of lowest-impact architectures while meeting all functional requirements.

items.text: Green IT initiatives include sustainable procurement criteria for technology acquisitions.

rubric.initial: Sustainability is not considered in technology procurement decisions.
rubric.basic: Some awareness of green procurement exists but criteria are not formally applied.
rubric.intermediate: Documented sustainable procurement criteria are applied to all significant technology acquisitions.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive green procurement scoring integrates environmental impact with total cost of ownership analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Automated procurement systems enforce sustainability requirements and track environmental impact of purchases.

items.text: An IT governance framework defines decision rights, escalation paths, and accountability structures.

rubric.initial: No formal IT governance framework exists; decisions are made ad hoc without clear authority.
rubric.basic: Some governance structures exist but decision rights are unclear and inconsistently applied.
rubric.intermediate: A formal governance framework documents decision rights, committees, and escalation procedures with regular reviews.
rubric.advanced: Governance is operationalized with automated workflows, clear RACI matrices, and performance metrics for decision quality.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive governance continuously evolves based on decision outcome analysis and organizational changes.

items.text: IT governance committees operate effectively with defined charters, membership, and cadence.

rubric.initial: No governance committees exist or they meet irregularly with no defined purpose.
rubric.basic: Committees exist but lack formal charters, have inconsistent attendance, or produce unclear outcomes.
rubric.intermediate: Governance committees have documented charters, regular cadence, defined membership, and tracked action items.
rubric.advanced: Committees leverage dashboards and analytics to make data-driven decisions with measurable impact on outcomes.
rubric.optimizing: Governance bodies operate with full transparency, predictive decision support, and continuous effectiveness assessment.

items.text: Governance processes ensure IT investments and policies comply with regulatory and organizational requirements.

rubric.initial: Compliance is not systematically addressed in IT governance; gaps are discovered reactively.
rubric.basic: Some compliance checks exist but are manual, inconsistent, and not integrated into governance workflows.
rubric.intermediate: Compliance requirements are mapped to governance processes with regular audits and documented evidence collection.
rubric.advanced: Automated compliance monitoring provides continuous assurance with real-time dashboards and exception management.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive compliance intelligence anticipates regulatory changes and proactively adjusts governance processes.

items.text: IT policies and standards are documented, communicated, and enforced across the organization.

rubric.initial: IT policies are undocumented or exist only informally with no enforcement.
rubric.basic: Some policies are documented but communication and enforcement are inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: A comprehensive policy framework is documented, communicated through training, and enforced via audits.
rubric.advanced: Policy compliance is monitored through automated controls with exception management and remediation tracking.
rubric.optimizing: Policies are dynamically updated based on threat intelligence, regulatory changes, and organizational learning.

items.text: Audit findings and governance exceptions are tracked to resolution with root cause analysis.

rubric.initial: Audit findings are not tracked systematically; repeat findings are common.
rubric.basic: Findings are logged but remediation tracking is manual and root cause analysis is rare.
rubric.intermediate: A formal process tracks all findings to resolution with documented root cause analysis and preventive actions.
rubric.advanced: Integrated GRC platforms automate finding management with trend analysis and automated remediation verification.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive analytics identify systemic governance weaknesses before they manifest as findings, enabling preemptive correction.

items.text: Governance effectiveness is regularly assessed and improved based on stakeholder feedback.

rubric.initial: Governance effectiveness is never assessed; structures persist regardless of value delivered.
rubric.basic: Informal feedback on governance exists but no structured assessment occurs.
rubric.intermediate: Annual governance effectiveness reviews use stakeholder surveys and process metrics to drive improvements.
rubric.advanced: Continuous governance improvement uses real-time feedback, benchmarking, and maturity assessments.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive governance automatically evolves based on organizational context, performance data, and best practices.

items.text: A structured innovation process captures, evaluates, and prioritizes ideas from across the organization.

rubric.initial: No formal innovation process exists; ideas surface randomly and are rarely pursued systematically.
rubric.basic: An informal suggestion mechanism exists but lacks evaluation criteria or follow-through processes.
rubric.intermediate: A documented innovation process includes idea submission, evaluation criteria, and prioritization with regular review.
rubric.advanced: Innovation pipelines are managed with stage-gate processes, business case validation, and portfolio-level tracking.
rubric.optimizing: AI-augmented idea management surfaces high-potential innovations, connects related concepts, and predicts market impact.

items.text: Dedicated time, funding, and resources are allocated to support innovation initiatives.

rubric.initial: No dedicated resources or funding exist for innovation; all capacity is consumed by operations.
rubric.basic: Innovation is encouraged informally but competes with operational work for unfunded time.
rubric.intermediate: A defined innovation budget and protected time allocation support experimentation and proof-of-concept development.
rubric.advanced: Innovation labs or teams operate with ring-fenced budgets, clear mandates, and measurable outcome expectations.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic innovation funding adjusts based on portfolio performance, market signals, and strategic opportunity assessment.

items.text: Innovation outcomes are measured and communicated to demonstrate value and sustain organizational support.

rubric.initial: Innovation outcomes are not tracked; there is no visibility into the return on innovation investment.
rubric.basic: Anecdotal success stories are shared but no systematic measurement of innovation outcomes exists.
rubric.intermediate: Innovation KPIs are defined and tracked, with regular reporting to leadership on outcomes and pipeline health.
rubric.advanced: Innovation ROI is measured across the portfolio with clear linkage to business value creation.
rubric.optimizing: Comprehensive innovation analytics benchmark against industry, track idea-to-value velocity, and optimize the innovation system.

items.text: The organization systematically monitors and evaluates emerging technologies for business relevance.

rubric.initial: Emerging technologies are adopted reactively based on vendor pressure or individual enthusiasm.
rubric.basic: Ad hoc technology scanning occurs but is not systematic or linked to business strategy.
rubric.intermediate: A technology radar process regularly evaluates emerging technologies against strategic relevance and readiness criteria.
rubric.advanced: Formal technology scouting integrates with strategic planning, including proof-of-concept programs and adoption roadmaps.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous horizon scanning with automated intelligence feeds drives proactive technology positioning ahead of competitors.

items.text: Proof-of-concept and pilot programs are used to de-risk emerging technology adoption.

rubric.initial: New technologies are adopted without piloting, leading to frequent failures and wasted investment.
rubric.basic: Occasional pilots occur but without structured evaluation criteria or clear paths to production.
rubric.intermediate: A documented PoC/pilot methodology defines success criteria, timelines, and go/no-go decision frameworks.
rubric.advanced: Pilot programs are integrated with portfolio management, with automated scaling paths from pilot to production.
rubric.optimizing: Rapid experimentation platforms enable continuous technology evaluation with automated impact assessment and scaling decisions.

items.text: Innovation management includes mechanisms to scale successful innovations across the organization.

rubric.initial: No mechanisms exist to scale innovations beyond their initial implementation.
rubric.basic: Occasional sharing of innovations occurs but scaling is ad hoc and unsupported.
rubric.intermediate: Documented processes support scaling successful innovations with resource allocation and change management.
rubric.advanced: Innovation scaling frameworks include playbooks, funding models, and organizational change support.
rubric.optimizing: Automated innovation scaling identifies replication opportunities and optimizes rollout strategies.

items.text: IT leaders demonstrate strategic thinking and effectively communicate the IT vision to all stakeholders.

rubric.initial: IT leadership is focused solely on operational firefighting with no strategic vision communicated.
rubric.basic: Some strategic vision exists but is not consistently communicated or understood by stakeholders.
rubric.intermediate: IT leaders regularly communicate a clear vision through documented channels with feedback mechanisms.
rubric.advanced: IT leaders are recognized as strategic partners who influence business direction and inspire teams with a compelling vision.
rubric.optimizing: IT leadership sets industry direction, cultivates thought leadership, and continuously adapts the vision based on foresight.

items.text: IT leaders develop and maintain strong relationships with business executives and key stakeholders.

rubric.initial: IT leaders operate in isolation with minimal interaction with business executives.
rubric.basic: Relationships exist but are transactional, focused on issue resolution rather than strategic partnership.
rubric.intermediate: IT leaders have regular engagement with business executives through defined forums and relationship management practices.
rubric.advanced: IT leaders are embedded in business decision-making with trusted advisor status and mutual goal-setting.
rubric.optimizing: IT leaders co-lead business transformation initiatives and are indistinguishable from business leaders in strategic influence.

items.text: IT leadership actively develops talent through mentoring, succession planning, and career development programs.

rubric.initial: No talent development programs exist; career growth in IT is undefined.
rubric.basic: Some training is available but there is no structured approach to talent development or succession planning.
rubric.intermediate: Formal talent development programs include career paths, mentoring, and succession planning for key roles.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive talent management integrates development, performance, and succession with personalized growth plans.
rubric.optimizing: Data-driven talent analytics predict future skill needs and proactively develop the workforce for emerging capabilities.

items.text: IT leaders foster a culture of collaboration, accountability, and continuous learning within the IT organization.

rubric.initial: The IT culture is siloed, blame-oriented, and resistant to change.
rubric.basic: Some cultural values are stated but not reinforced through leadership behavior or organizational practices.
rubric.intermediate: Leaders model desired behaviors and reinforce culture through documented values, recognition programs, and team practices.
rubric.advanced: Culture metrics are measured regularly, with leadership actively addressing gaps and celebrating improvements.
rubric.optimizing: A self-reinforcing culture of excellence attracts top talent and drives continuous innovation from within.

items.text: IT leadership development programs build capabilities in digital transformation and business partnership.

rubric.initial: No IT leadership development programs exist focused on digital or business capabilities.
rubric.basic: Some training is available but not systematically targeted at leadership capability gaps.
rubric.intermediate: Structured leadership programs develop digital transformation, strategic thinking, and business partnership skills.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive leadership academies include executive coaching, rotations, and action learning projects.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive leadership development uses competency analytics to personalize development paths for each leader.

items.text: Change readiness assessments are conducted before major IT initiatives to identify risks and resistance.

rubric.initial: Changes are implemented without assessing organizational readiness, leading to frequent resistance and failure.
rubric.basic: Informal readiness assessments are occasionally performed but are not standardized or comprehensive.
rubric.intermediate: Formal readiness assessments are conducted for all major initiatives using documented frameworks and tools.
rubric.advanced: Readiness data drives tailored change strategies with predictive models for adoption risk and targeted interventions.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous organizational sensing provides real-time readiness intelligence, enabling proactive change preparation.

items.text: Adoption frameworks ensure that IT changes achieve intended utilization and benefit realization.

rubric.initial: No adoption tracking exists; changes are deployed with no follow-up on actual usage.
rubric.basic: Basic usage metrics are collected post-deployment but not linked to benefit realization.
rubric.intermediate: Adoption frameworks define success metrics, track utilization, and include remediation plans for low adoption.
rubric.advanced: Adoption analytics provide granular insights into user behavior with automated nudges and personalized support.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive adoption models enable pre-emptive interventions, achieving consistently high adoption rates across all initiatives.

items.text: Communication plans effectively prepare stakeholders for upcoming changes and maintain engagement throughout transitions.

rubric.initial: Change communication is absent or occurs only after implementation, causing confusion and resistance.
rubric.basic: Some communication occurs but is inconsistent, generic, and not tailored to stakeholder groups.
rubric.intermediate: Structured communication plans segment audiences and deliver targeted messaging through appropriate channels.
rubric.advanced: Multi-channel communication campaigns use feedback loops to adjust messaging and address concerns in real time.
rubric.optimizing: Personalized, data-driven communication achieves measurable engagement and sentiment improvement across all stakeholder groups.

items.text: The organization develops change management capabilities as a core competency across IT and business teams.

rubric.initial: Change management is not recognized as a discipline; there are no trained practitioners.
rubric.basic: A few individuals have change management skills but there is no organizational capability-building effort.
rubric.intermediate: Change management training is available, and a community of practice shares methods and lessons learned.
rubric.advanced: Certified change management practitioners are embedded in all major programs with a center of excellence providing governance.
rubric.optimizing: Change agility is a core organizational competency, with all leaders and teams equipped to drive and sustain change.

items.text: Lessons learned from past change initiatives are captured and applied to improve future change outcomes.

rubric.initial: No post-change reviews are conducted; the same mistakes are repeated across initiatives.
rubric.basic: Occasional retrospectives occur but insights are not captured systematically or applied to future work.
rubric.intermediate: Post-implementation reviews are standard practice with documented lessons learned fed into process improvements.
rubric.advanced: A knowledge base of change patterns and anti-patterns is actively maintained and referenced in planning.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven pattern recognition across the change portfolio identifies systemic improvement opportunities and predicts change risks.

items.text: Change management maturity is assessed and improved to increase organizational agility.

rubric.initial: Change management maturity is not assessed; approaches are ad hoc.
rubric.basic: Informal recognition of change management needs exists but maturity is not measured.
rubric.intermediate: Periodic change management maturity assessments drive targeted capability improvements.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive maturity assessments benchmark against industry standards with structured improvement roadmaps.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous maturity monitoring drives adaptive improvement in organizational change capability.

items.text: The IT organizational structure is designed to support strategic objectives and enable effective service delivery.

rubric.initial: The IT structure has evolved organically with no deliberate design aligned to strategy.
rubric.basic: The structure is documented but has not been reviewed against strategic objectives or operating model needs.
rubric.intermediate: The organizational structure is periodically reviewed and adjusted to align with strategic priorities and delivery models.
rubric.advanced: The structure is optimized for agility with clear design principles, regular assessment, and deliberate evolution.
rubric.optimizing: The organization continuously adapts its structure using workforce analytics and scenario modeling to stay ahead of strategic needs.

items.text: Roles, responsibilities, and reporting lines are clearly defined and understood across the IT organization.

rubric.initial: Roles are ambiguous with significant overlap, gaps, and confusion about responsibilities.
rubric.basic: Job descriptions exist for some roles but are outdated and do not reflect actual responsibilities.
rubric.intermediate: All roles have current descriptions with clear responsibilities, and RACI matrices define cross-functional accountabilities.
rubric.advanced: Role clarity is maintained through integrated HR systems with skills-based role definitions and dynamic team composition.
rubric.optimizing: AI-assisted workforce planning continuously optimizes role design and team composition based on workload patterns and skill availability.

items.text: IT workforce planning identifies future skill needs and develops strategies to address capability gaps.

rubric.initial: No workforce planning exists; hiring is purely reactive to immediate needs.
rubric.basic: Basic headcount planning occurs annually but does not address future skill requirements.
rubric.intermediate: Workforce plans identify skill gaps based on strategic roadmaps with documented build, buy, and borrow strategies.
rubric.advanced: Skills inventories and gap analysis are integrated with learning platforms and talent acquisition for proactive capability building.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive workforce analytics anticipate skill needs years ahead, with continuous adaptation of development and hiring strategies.

items.text: The organization maintains the right balance of internal, contract, and outsourced IT resources.

rubric.initial: Resource sourcing decisions are ad hoc with no strategy for balancing internal and external talent.
rubric.basic: A general preference exists but there is no formal framework for sourcing decisions.
rubric.intermediate: A documented sourcing strategy defines criteria for internal, contract, and outsourced resources with regular review.
rubric.advanced: Dynamic resource management optimizes the talent mix based on cost, capability, risk, and strategic importance.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven talent orchestration continuously optimizes the workforce blend across all sourcing channels and geographies.

items.text: Organizational design considers digital operating model requirements and agile ways of working.

rubric.initial: Organizational design does not account for digital operating models or agile practices.
rubric.basic: Some teams have adopted agile structures but the overall organization design is traditional.
rubric.intermediate: Organizational design explicitly incorporates digital operating models with documented agile structures.
rubric.advanced: Hybrid organizational models balance agile delivery teams with stable platform and governance structures.
rubric.optimizing: Continuously evolving organizational design adapts to emerging digital operating model patterns and market demands.

items.text: IT risks are systematically identified through regular assessments covering technology, operational, and strategic dimensions.

rubric.initial: IT risks are not formally identified; issues are discovered only when they materialize as incidents.
rubric.basic: Some risk identification occurs but is ad hoc, incomplete, and focused only on obvious technology risks.
rubric.intermediate: Regular risk assessments use documented methodologies covering technology, operational, and strategic risk categories.
rubric.advanced: Continuous risk sensing combines automated scanning, threat intelligence, and business context for comprehensive identification.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive risk analytics use machine learning to identify emerging risks before they manifest, enabling preemptive action.

items.text: Risks are assessed for likelihood and impact using consistent criteria and documented in a risk register.

rubric.initial: No risk assessment methodology or register exists.
rubric.basic: A risk register exists but assessments are subjective and criteria are inconsistently applied.
rubric.intermediate: Risks are assessed using standardized likelihood and impact scales with a maintained register reviewed regularly.
rubric.advanced: Quantitative risk analysis supplements qualitative assessment, with Monte Carlo simulations for high-impact scenarios.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic risk scoring automatically adjusts based on real-time indicators, external intelligence, and control effectiveness data.

items.text: Risk appetite and tolerance levels are defined and guide IT decision-making.

rubric.initial: Risk appetite is undefined; risk acceptance decisions are inconsistent and undocumented.
rubric.basic: General risk appetite statements exist but are not translated into actionable tolerance levels for IT.
rubric.intermediate: Documented risk appetite and tolerance levels are aligned with business objectives and applied in IT decision-making.
rubric.advanced: Risk appetite is operationalized through automated guardrails that flag decisions exceeding tolerance thresholds.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic risk appetite adjusts based on organizational context, market conditions, and portfolio performance.

items.text: Risk mitigation plans are developed, funded, and tracked for all significant IT risks.

rubric.initial: No formal mitigation plans exist; risks are accepted by default through inaction.
rubric.basic: Mitigation plans exist for some high-profile risks but are not consistently funded or tracked.
rubric.intermediate: All significant risks have documented mitigation plans with assigned owners, budgets, and tracked milestones.
rubric.advanced: Mitigation effectiveness is measured and plans are dynamically adjusted based on residual risk monitoring.
rubric.optimizing: Automated risk response orchestration triggers pre-approved mitigation actions and continuously optimizes risk treatment costs.

items.text: Key risk indicators are monitored and reported to leadership on a regular basis.

rubric.initial: No risk monitoring or reporting to leadership exists.
rubric.basic: Periodic risk reports are produced but lack timeliness, depth, or actionable insights.
rubric.intermediate: Defined KRIs are monitored and reported to leadership through standardized risk dashboards on a regular cadence.
rubric.advanced: Real-time risk dashboards provide leadership with drill-down capabilities, trend analysis, and automated escalation.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive risk intelligence provides forward-looking risk assessments that enable proactive strategic adjustments.

items.text: Risk appetite statements guide risk-taking decisions and are aligned with business strategy.

rubric.initial: No risk appetite statements exist; risk tolerance is undefined.
rubric.basic: Informal risk tolerance exists but is not documented or aligned with strategy.
rubric.intermediate: Documented risk appetite statements guide IT risk decisions with alignment to business strategy.
rubric.advanced: Quantified risk appetite frameworks with defined thresholds inform portfolio and operational decisions.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic risk appetite adjusts based on business context, market conditions, and organizational resilience metrics.

items.text: IT service performance is measured through a comprehensive set of metrics aligned with business outcomes.

rubric.initial: No service performance metrics are defined or collected.
rubric.basic: Basic technical metrics are collected (uptime, response time) but not linked to business outcomes.
rubric.intermediate: A balanced set of service metrics covers technical performance, user satisfaction, and business impact.
rubric.advanced: Service analytics integrate multiple data sources to provide end-to-end visibility from infrastructure to business outcome.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive service analytics anticipate degradation and business impact before users are affected.

items.text: Service level performance is tracked against agreements and triggers corrective action when thresholds are breached.

rubric.initial: SLAs are not defined or measured; service performance is unknown.
rubric.basic: SLAs exist for some services but monitoring is manual and inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: All major services have SLAs with automated monitoring, regular reporting, and documented escalation procedures.
rubric.advanced: SLA management is integrated with service operations, triggering automated remediation and proactive communication.
rubric.optimizing: Experience-level agreements supplement SLAs, with continuous optimization of service targets based on business value.

items.text: Analytics capabilities transform raw service data into actionable insights for decision-makers.

rubric.initial: Raw data is available but no analytics capability transforms it into useful insights.
rubric.basic: Basic reporting provides historical views but analysis is manual and infrequent.
rubric.intermediate: Self-service analytics tools provide stakeholders with dashboards and standard reports on service performance.
rubric.advanced: Advanced analytics including trend analysis, correlation, and root cause identification support proactive decision-making.
rubric.optimizing: AI-powered analytics provide prescriptive recommendations and automated decision support across all service domains.

items.text: Service intelligence drives continuous improvement in IT service delivery and customer experience.

rubric.initial: Service improvement is reactive and not informed by data or analytics.
rubric.basic: Some data is used to justify improvements but analysis is superficial and inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Regular service reviews use analytics to identify improvement opportunities with tracked action items.
rubric.advanced: Closed-loop analytics drive a systematic improvement engine with measurable impact on service quality and experience.
rubric.optimizing: Autonomous service optimization uses AI to continuously improve service delivery, predict needs, and personalize experiences.

items.text: Analytics capabilities include prescriptive and cognitive analytics for advanced decision support.

rubric.initial: No analytics capabilities exist beyond basic reporting.
rubric.basic: Descriptive analytics provide historical insights but predictive capabilities are absent.
rubric.intermediate: Predictive analytics models support forecasting with documented accuracy metrics.
rubric.advanced: Prescriptive analytics recommend optimal actions based on predictive models and constraint analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Cognitive analytics autonomously identify patterns, generate insights, and recommend actions across domains.

items.text: A sourcing strategy defines how IT selects and engages vendors aligned with organizational objectives.

rubric.initial: No sourcing strategy exists; vendor selection is ad hoc and driven by individual preferences.
rubric.basic: General sourcing guidelines exist but are not consistently followed across the IT organization.
rubric.intermediate: A documented sourcing strategy defines evaluation criteria, preferred vendor tiers, and engagement models.
rubric.advanced: Strategic sourcing integrates with enterprise procurement, using total cost of ownership and value-based evaluation.
rubric.optimizing: AI-augmented sourcing intelligence identifies optimal vendor combinations and predicts market shifts for proactive positioning.

items.text: Contracts are managed throughout their lifecycle with clear terms, obligations, and renewal processes.

rubric.initial: Contracts are filed and forgotten; obligations and renewal dates are frequently missed.
rubric.basic: Basic contract tracking exists but lifecycle management is incomplete and renewal planning is reactive.
rubric.intermediate: Contract lifecycle management processes are documented with automated renewal alerts and obligation tracking.
rubric.advanced: Integrated CLM platforms manage all vendor contracts with compliance monitoring, obligation fulfillment, and value realization tracking.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent contract management uses NLP to analyze terms, benchmark against market, and optimize negotiation positions.

items.text: Vendor risk is assessed and managed as part of the sourcing and contract management process.

rubric.initial: Vendor risk is not assessed; the organization is unaware of its exposure through third parties.
rubric.basic: Basic due diligence is performed at onboarding but ongoing risk monitoring is absent.
rubric.intermediate: Vendor risk assessments are conducted at onboarding and periodically, covering financial, operational, and security dimensions.
rubric.advanced: Continuous vendor risk monitoring integrates with enterprise risk management and triggers automated response actions.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive vendor risk analytics identify emerging risks across the supply chain and recommend preemptive diversification.

items.text: Supplier performance is measured against defined KPIs and reviewed regularly to ensure value delivery.

rubric.initial: Supplier performance is not measured; there is no visibility into whether vendors deliver expected value.
rubric.basic: Some performance data is collected but reviews are informal and do not drive accountability.
rubric.intermediate: Supplier scorecards with defined KPIs are reviewed in regular business reviews with documented action plans.
rubric.advanced: Automated performance monitoring provides real-time supplier scorecards with trend analysis and benchmarking.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive supplier analytics identify performance trends early and trigger proactive engagement to maintain service excellence.

items.text: Strategic supplier relationships are managed as partnerships with joint planning and mutual value creation.

rubric.initial: All vendor relationships are transactional with no differentiation between strategic and commodity suppliers.
rubric.basic: Strategic suppliers are identified but relationship management does not differ from standard vendors.
rubric.intermediate: Strategic suppliers have dedicated relationship managers, joint governance, and collaborative planning sessions.
rubric.advanced: Joint innovation programs with strategic suppliers drive mutual value creation with shared roadmaps and co-investment.
rubric.optimizing: Ecosystem orchestration manages a network of strategic partners, driving collaborative innovation and shared competitive advantage.

items.text: Supplier ecosystem management creates strategic value through partnership and co-innovation.

rubric.initial: Suppliers are managed transactionally with no consideration of strategic partnership value.
rubric.basic: Some key suppliers are treated as partners but co-innovation is rare.
rubric.intermediate: Strategic supplier partnerships include joint planning, innovation sharing, and mutual value creation.
rubric.advanced: A managed supplier ecosystem drives co-innovation with structured value sharing and intellectual property frameworks.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic ecosystem orchestration continuously optimizes supplier partnerships based on value delivery and market evolution.

items.text: The organization has a documented IT strategy aligned with business objectives.

rubric.initial: No formal IT strategy exists. IT decisions are ad hoc and reactive.
rubric.basic: A basic IT strategy document exists but is not consistently aligned with business goals.
rubric.intermediate: IT strategy is documented, aligned with business objectives, and reviewed periodically.
rubric.advanced: IT strategy is fully integrated with business strategy, with formal alignment processes and regular reviews.
rubric.optimizing: IT strategy co-evolves with business strategy through continuous feedback loops and predictive analytics.

items.text: IT strategic goals are translated into measurable objectives with clear ownership and timelines.

rubric.initial: Strategic goals are vague aspirations with no measurable objectives or accountability.
rubric.basic: Some objectives are defined but lack clear metrics, ownership, or realistic timelines.
rubric.intermediate: Strategic goals are decomposed into SMART objectives with assigned owners and tracked milestones.
rubric.advanced: Objectives are cascaded through the organization with automated progress tracking and variance reporting.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic objective management adjusts targets based on real-time performance data and changing business conditions.

items.text: The IT vision and mission are clearly articulated and understood at all levels of the IT organization.

rubric.initial: No IT vision or mission statement exists; the purpose of IT is unclear beyond keeping systems running.
rubric.basic: A vision statement exists but is not well-known or understood beyond senior IT leadership.
rubric.intermediate: The IT vision and mission are documented, communicated regularly, and referenced in planning and decision-making.
rubric.advanced: The vision is deeply embedded in IT culture, influencing behavior and guiding decisions at all levels.
rubric.optimizing: The IT vision inspires innovation and is recognized externally as a model of IT-business partnership.

items.text: Strategy execution is tracked through a balanced set of metrics covering financial, operational, and innovation dimensions.

rubric.initial: No strategy execution metrics exist; there is no way to assess whether the strategy is on track.
rubric.basic: A few financial metrics are tracked but operational and innovation dimensions are not measured.
rubric.intermediate: A balanced scorecard or equivalent framework tracks execution across multiple dimensions with regular reviews.
rubric.advanced: Integrated strategy dashboards provide real-time execution visibility with automated alerts for off-track initiatives.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive execution analytics identify at-risk initiatives early and recommend corrective actions based on pattern analysis.

items.text: Strategic initiatives are managed through a portfolio approach with governance, prioritization, and resource allocation.

rubric.initial: Strategic initiatives are managed independently with no portfolio-level coordination or governance.
rubric.basic: Some portfolio visibility exists but prioritization and resource allocation are not integrated.
rubric.intermediate: A strategic portfolio management process governs prioritization, resource allocation, and progress tracking.
rubric.advanced: Portfolio management integrates with financial planning and resource management for optimized strategic investment.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven portfolio optimization continuously rebalances investments based on value realization, risk, and strategic fit.

items.text: The organization conducts regular strategy reviews and adapts plans based on internal performance and external changes.

rubric.initial: Strategy is set once and never revisited; plans quickly become irrelevant.
rubric.basic: Annual strategy reviews occur but are superficial and do not result in meaningful plan adjustments.
rubric.intermediate: Quarterly strategy reviews assess performance against objectives and incorporate environmental changes into updated plans.
rubric.advanced: Continuous strategy sensing monitors internal and external signals, triggering timely strategic pivots when warranted.
rubric.optimizing: The organization operates with strategic agility, using real-time intelligence to continuously evolve its strategic positioning.

items.text: Strategic planning incorporates external ecosystem and platform strategy considerations.

rubric.initial: Strategic planning is internally focused with no consideration of external ecosystems.
rubric.basic: External factors are acknowledged but ecosystem strategy is not formally developed.
rubric.intermediate: Strategic plans explicitly address platform and ecosystem strategies with defined partnership approaches.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive ecosystem strategies guide platform development, API strategies, and partnership portfolios.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive ecosystem strategies evolve continuously based on market dynamics and network effect analytics.

items.text: IT budgets are developed through a structured planning process aligned with organizational strategy.

rubric.initial: IT budgets are created reactively with no formal planning process or strategic alignment.
rubric.basic: A basic annual budgeting process exists but lacks rigorous strategic alignment or stakeholder input.
rubric.intermediate: Budget planning follows a documented methodology with strategic alignment and cross-functional input.
rubric.advanced: Rolling budgets are maintained with scenario modeling, strategic linkage, and executive governance.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous budget optimization uses predictive analytics, real-time market data, and dynamic strategic alignment.

items.text: Budget allocations reflect prioritized business needs and are transparent to stakeholders.

rubric.initial: Budget allocations are opaque, historically based, and disconnected from business priorities.
rubric.basic: Some allocation rationale exists but is not consistently communicated or priority-driven.
rubric.intermediate: Allocations use a documented prioritization framework with stakeholder visibility into decisions.
rubric.advanced: Dynamic allocation models adjust funding based on shifting business priorities with full transparency.
rubric.optimizing: AI-assisted allocation engines optimize funding distribution in real time based on value delivery metrics.

items.text: Capital and operational expenditure categories are clearly defined and managed separately.

rubric.initial: No distinction is made between capital and operational IT expenditure.
rubric.basic: CapEx and OpEx are distinguished but categorization is inconsistent across the organization.
rubric.intermediate: Clear policies govern CapEx/OpEx classification with consistent application and regular review.
rubric.advanced: CapEx/OpEx management is integrated with financial planning tools and cloud consumption models.
rubric.optimizing: Automated classification engines handle CapEx/OpEx categorization with continuous compliance monitoring.

items.text: Budget contingency reserves are maintained to address unforeseen IT needs.

rubric.initial: No contingency reserves exist; unplanned costs result in budget overruns or project cancellations.
rubric.basic: Informal contingency buffers exist but are not formally managed or governed.
rubric.intermediate: Documented contingency reserves are maintained with clear criteria for access and replenishment.
rubric.advanced: Risk-adjusted contingency models are used, with reserves tied to portfolio risk profiles.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive risk analytics dynamically size contingency reserves based on real-time project and market data.

items.text: Zero-based budgeting principles are applied to challenge legacy spending patterns.

rubric.initial: Budgets are entirely incremental, based on prior-year spending with no justification required.
rubric.basic: Occasional zero-based reviews are conducted for selected areas but the approach is not systematic.
rubric.intermediate: Periodic zero-based budgeting exercises are conducted for all IT cost categories with documented outcomes.
rubric.advanced: Annual zero-based reviews are integrated into the budget cycle with automated baseline analysis tools.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous zero-based optimization challenges all spending with AI-driven value-per-dollar analysis.

items.text: Multi-year budget planning aligns IT investment horizons with strategic planning cycles.

rubric.initial: IT budgets are planned only one year at a time with no multi-year perspective.
rubric.basic: Some multi-year projections exist but are not formally integrated with strategic plans.
rubric.intermediate: Three-to-five year budget plans align with strategic planning cycles and are updated annually.
rubric.advanced: Rolling multi-year budgets integrate with strategic roadmaps and adapt to portfolio changes.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous long-range financial planning uses scenario modeling to optimize multi-year investment strategies.

items.text: Budget planning processes incorporate lessons learned from prior budget cycles to improve accuracy.

rubric.initial: No retrospective analysis of budget accuracy is performed.
rubric.basic: Major budget misses are noted but not systematically analyzed for improvement.
rubric.intermediate: Post-cycle budget reviews identify accuracy issues and feed improvements into the next planning cycle.
rubric.advanced: Statistical analysis of historical budget accuracy drives systematic improvement in estimation techniques.
rubric.optimizing: Machine learning models continuously improve budget estimation accuracy based on historical patterns.

items.text: Actual IT spending is tracked against budget with regular variance reporting.

rubric.initial: Spending is not tracked against budget in any systematic way.
rubric.basic: Quarterly or annual comparisons of actual vs. budget are performed but lack detail.
rubric.intermediate: Monthly variance reports are produced with documented explanations for significant deviations.
rubric.advanced: Real-time budget tracking dashboards provide drill-down variance analysis with automated alerts.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive variance analytics forecast future deviations and trigger proactive corrective actions.

items.text: Root-cause analysis is performed for material budget variances and corrective actions are tracked.

rubric.initial: Budget variances are noted but no root-cause analysis or corrective action is taken.
rubric.basic: Ad hoc investigation occurs for large variances but findings are not systematically documented.
rubric.intermediate: A formal root-cause analysis process exists with documented corrective actions and follow-up.
rubric.advanced: Variance patterns are analyzed across periods to identify systemic issues and drive process improvements.
rubric.optimizing: Machine learning models identify variance root causes in real time and recommend targeted interventions.

items.text: Budget forecasts are updated regularly to reflect changing conditions and improve accuracy.

rubric.initial: Budget forecasts are set once annually and never revised regardless of changing conditions.
rubric.basic: Forecasts are updated occasionally but the process is informal and inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Quarterly forecast updates are performed using a structured methodology with documented assumptions.
rubric.advanced: Monthly rolling forecasts incorporate actuals, pipeline changes, and market intelligence.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous forecasting engines produce real-time projections with confidence intervals and scenario analysis.

items.text: IT budget information is communicated effectively to business stakeholders at appropriate levels of detail.

rubric.initial: IT budget information is not shared with business stakeholders.
rubric.basic: High-level budget summaries are shared annually but lack actionable detail.
rubric.intermediate: Tailored budget communications are provided to different stakeholder groups with appropriate detail and frequency.
rubric.advanced: Interactive budget portals enable stakeholders to explore IT financial data relevant to their areas.
rubric.optimizing: Personalized financial insights are delivered proactively to stakeholders with context-aware recommendations.

items.text: Business units participate in IT budget planning to ensure alignment with their priorities.

rubric.initial: Business units have no input into IT budget planning.
rubric.basic: Business units provide wish lists but have limited influence on actual budget decisions.
rubric.intermediate: Structured collaboration processes ensure business unit priorities are reflected in IT budget allocations.
rubric.advanced: Joint IT-business planning teams co-create budgets with shared accountability for outcomes.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous business-IT financial dialogue dynamically adjusts priorities and allocations throughout the year.

items.text: Budget trade-off decisions are transparent and communicated with clear rationale.

rubric.initial: Budget decisions are made without explanation or transparency.
rubric.basic: Major budget decisions are communicated but rationale is limited or unclear.
rubric.intermediate: Trade-off decisions are documented with clear rationale and communicated to affected stakeholders.
rubric.advanced: Decision frameworks make trade-off criteria explicit, with impact analysis shared across the organization.
rubric.optimizing: Interactive scenario tools allow stakeholders to understand trade-offs and contribute to optimization decisions.

items.text: IT financial literacy programs help business stakeholders understand and engage with IT budgets.

rubric.initial: No IT financial literacy initiatives exist for business stakeholders.
rubric.basic: Ad hoc explanations are provided when stakeholders request budget clarification.
rubric.intermediate: Structured financial literacy programs help key stakeholders understand IT cost structures and drivers.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive training and self-service tools build broad organizational capability in IT financial management.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive learning programs personalize IT financial education based on role and engagement patterns.

items.text: IT budget performance is tied to executive accountability with clear ownership at all levels.

rubric.initial: No accountability exists for IT budget performance outcomes.
rubric.basic: CIO has nominal budget responsibility but accountability is not cascaded.
rubric.intermediate: Budget accountability is assigned at multiple levels with defined performance expectations.
rubric.advanced: Budget performance is integrated into performance management with incentives and consequences.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic accountability models adapt responsibilities based on organizational changes and portfolio shifts.

items.text: Budget processes support rapid reallocation of funds in response to emerging opportunities or threats.

rubric.initial: Budget reallocation requires lengthy approval cycles and is rarely accomplished mid-year.
rubric.basic: Emergency reallocations are possible but the process is slow and cumbersome.
rubric.intermediate: Defined fast-track reallocation processes enable timely funding shifts within governance guardrails.
rubric.advanced: Agile budgeting practices enable rapid reallocation with delegated authority and real-time visibility.
rubric.optimizing: Autonomous budget rebalancing responds to value signals with minimal human intervention within policy bounds.

items.text: Agile funding models support iterative delivery approaches with incremental investment decisions.

rubric.initial: All funding is committed upfront in large blocks with no iterative funding options.
rubric.basic: Some projects receive phased funding but the approach is not standardized.
rubric.intermediate: Agile funding models provide incremental investments tied to value delivery milestones.
rubric.advanced: Lean portfolio budgeting aligns funding with value streams and empowers teams with delegated spending authority.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous funding optimization adjusts investment levels dynamically based on team velocity and value delivery.

items.text: Budget flexibility mechanisms accommodate experimentation and innovation without jeopardizing core operations.

rubric.initial: No budget flexibility exists for experimentation; all funds are committed to planned activities.
rubric.basic: Small discretionary funds exist but are not formally managed for innovation purposes.
rubric.intermediate: Dedicated innovation budgets with appropriate governance support controlled experimentation.
rubric.advanced: Portfolio-level flexibility reserves fund emergent opportunities with rapid evaluation and approval processes.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic budget ecosystems balance stability and agility, continuously optimizing the innovation investment mix.

items.text: Financial controls are in place to ensure IT spending adheres to approved budgets and policies.

rubric.initial: No formal financial controls govern IT spending; approvals are ad hoc or absent.
rubric.basic: Basic approval workflows exist but controls are inconsistently applied and easily bypassed.
rubric.intermediate: Documented financial controls with approval hierarchies are enforced across IT spending categories.
rubric.advanced: Automated controls enforce spending policies in real time with exception management and audit trails.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent controls adapt thresholds dynamically based on risk profiles and spending patterns.

items.text: Financial governance structures provide oversight of IT budget performance at appropriate management levels.

rubric.initial: No governance structure exists for IT financial oversight.
rubric.basic: Senior management reviews IT financials occasionally but without a formal governance structure.
rubric.intermediate: A defined governance body reviews IT budget performance on a regular schedule with documented terms of reference.
rubric.advanced: Multi-tier governance provides strategic, tactical, and operational oversight with clear escalation paths.
rubric.optimizing: Governance is augmented by real-time analytics dashboards enabling proactive intervention and continuous improvement.

items.text: Audit processes verify the accuracy and compliance of IT financial reporting.

rubric.initial: IT financial data is not subject to any audit or verification process.
rubric.basic: Periodic audits occur but scope is limited and findings are not systematically addressed.
rubric.intermediate: Regular internal audits cover IT financials with documented findings and remediation tracking.
rubric.advanced: Continuous auditing techniques supplement periodic audits with automated compliance checking.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven continuous assurance monitors IT financial data integrity and compliance in real time.

items.text: Segregation of duties is maintained in IT financial processes to reduce fraud risk.

rubric.initial: No segregation of duties exists in IT financial processes.
rubric.basic: Some separation of responsibilities exists but is informal and not consistently enforced.
rubric.intermediate: Documented segregation of duties policies are enforced with periodic compliance reviews.
rubric.advanced: Automated role-based access controls enforce segregation of duties with conflict detection.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic duty segregation adapts to organizational changes with continuous monitoring and anomaly detection.

items.text: Financial risk management processes identify and mitigate IT budget-related risks.

rubric.initial: IT financial risks are not identified or managed.
rubric.basic: Major financial risks are known informally but not systematically assessed or mitigated.
rubric.intermediate: A financial risk register is maintained with documented assessment and mitigation plans.
