rubric.advanced: Quantitative risk analysis models assess financial exposure with scenario-based mitigation strategies.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time financial risk monitoring detects emerging threats and triggers automated mitigation responses.

items.text: IT financial performance is reported using standardized KPIs aligned with business objectives.

rubric.initial: No standardized KPIs exist for IT financial performance reporting.
rubric.basic: Some financial metrics are tracked but KPIs are not standardized or aligned to business goals.
rubric.intermediate: A defined set of financial KPIs is reported regularly with clear linkage to business objectives.
rubric.advanced: KPI dashboards provide real-time visibility with trend analysis and benchmarking against peers.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive KPI models forecast financial performance and recommend optimization actions.

items.text: Return on IT investments is tracked and reported to demonstrate value delivery.

rubric.initial: ROI is not measured for IT investments.
rubric.basic: ROI is estimated for major projects at inception but rarely tracked post-implementation.
rubric.intermediate: Post-implementation ROI reviews are conducted for significant investments with documented results.
rubric.advanced: Ongoing ROI tracking is integrated into investment lifecycle management with variance analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time value dashboards track ROI continuously with predictive models for future returns.

items.text: Benchmarking of IT costs against industry standards informs budget performance evaluation.

rubric.initial: No external benchmarking of IT costs is performed.
rubric.basic: Occasional benchmarking is done using publicly available data but lacks rigor.
rubric.intermediate: Structured benchmarking against industry peers is performed annually using recognized frameworks.
rubric.advanced: Continuous benchmarking integrates multiple data sources with normalization for organizational context.
rubric.optimizing: Automated benchmarking platforms provide real-time peer comparisons with actionable improvement recommendations.

items.text: Total cost of IT services is measured and reported to enable value-for-money assessments.

rubric.initial: Total cost of IT services is unknown; only direct costs are partially tracked.
rubric.basic: Direct costs are known for major services but fully loaded costs are not calculated.
rubric.intermediate: Fully loaded costs are calculated for all major IT services using documented allocation methods.
rubric.advanced: Automated service costing provides real-time fully loaded costs with allocation transparency.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic service costing models predict cost evolution and optimize service delivery economics.

items.text: Financial performance trends are analyzed to identify efficiency improvements and cost reduction opportunities.

rubric.initial: No trend analysis of IT financial performance is conducted.
rubric.basic: Year-over-year cost comparisons exist but trend analysis is superficial.
rubric.intermediate: Multi-year trend analysis identifies cost drivers and efficiency improvement opportunities.
rubric.advanced: Advanced trend analytics with normalization for growth factors reveal underlying efficiency patterns.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive trend models forecast cost trajectories and automatically identify optimization opportunities.

items.text: IT service costs are transparent and attributable to consuming business units.

rubric.initial: IT costs are opaque; business units have no visibility into the cost of services they consume.
rubric.basic: High-level cost allocations exist but do not reflect actual service consumption.
rubric.intermediate: Service-level costing provides transparent cost attribution based on defined consumption metrics.
rubric.advanced: Automated showback systems provide real-time cost visibility to business units with drill-down capability.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive cost models help business units forecast and optimize their IT consumption proactively.

items.text: IT service catalogs include cost information to enable informed demand decisions.

rubric.initial: No IT service catalog exists or catalog entries lack cost information.
rubric.basic: Some services have associated costs but the catalog is incomplete and costs are outdated.
rubric.intermediate: A comprehensive service catalog includes current unit costs and pricing models for all services.
rubric.advanced: Dynamic pricing in the service catalog reflects actual costs with volume discounts and tier options.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent catalog systems recommend cost-optimal service configurations based on usage patterns.

items.text: Chargeback or showback mechanisms drive cost-conscious behavior among IT consumers.

rubric.initial: No chargeback or showback mechanisms exist; IT is treated as free by consumers.
rubric.basic: Basic showback reports are produced but have limited impact on consumption behavior.
rubric.intermediate: Effective showback or chargeback mechanisms are in place with documented impact on demand management.
rubric.advanced: Sophisticated chargeback models with consumption-based pricing drive measurable cost optimization behavior.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic pricing and gamified cost management encourage continuous optimization with organizational rewards.

items.text: Unit cost metrics for IT services are tracked and benchmarked to drive efficiency.

rubric.initial: Unit costs for IT services are unknown.
rubric.basic: Some unit costs are calculated but methodology is inconsistent and benchmarking is absent.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized unit cost metrics are calculated for all major services with annual benchmarking.
rubric.advanced: Continuous unit cost tracking with peer benchmarking drives targeted efficiency improvement programs.
rubric.optimizing: Automated unit cost optimization identifies improvement opportunities and models efficiency interventions.

items.text: Financial analytics provide actionable insights that improve IT spending decisions.

rubric.initial: No financial analytics capability exists for IT spending.
rubric.basic: Basic spending reports are produced but analytical insights are limited.
rubric.intermediate: Financial analytics identify spending patterns, anomalies, and optimization opportunities.
rubric.advanced: Advanced analytics including predictive modeling inform proactive spending decisions.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven financial intelligence continuously generates actionable recommendations for spending optimization.

items.text: Financial management processes are regularly reviewed and improved based on effectiveness metrics.

rubric.initial: Financial management processes are static and never reviewed for effectiveness.
rubric.basic: Occasional process improvements are made reactively when problems arise.
rubric.intermediate: Annual reviews evaluate financial process effectiveness with documented improvement actions.
rubric.advanced: Continuous improvement programs use process mining and efficiency metrics to optimize financial operations.
rubric.optimizing: Self-optimizing financial processes leverage automation and AI to continuously improve efficiency and accuracy.

items.text: Financial automation reduces manual effort and improves accuracy in IT budget management.

rubric.initial: IT financial processes are entirely manual with spreadsheet-based tracking.
rubric.basic: Some automation exists for reporting but most processes remain manual.
rubric.intermediate: Key financial processes are automated including reporting, approvals, and reconciliation.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive financial automation covers end-to-end processes with exception-based human intervention.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent automation with RPA and AI handles complex financial operations with continuous learning.

items.text: Financial management capabilities are developed through training and competency programs.

rubric.initial: No financial management training exists for IT leaders or managers.
rubric.basic: Basic financial training is available but participation is voluntary and infrequent.
rubric.intermediate: Structured financial competency programs develop IT leaders' budget management capabilities.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive finance-for-IT programs include simulations, case studies, and certification paths.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive learning platforms personalize financial skill development based on role needs and performance gaps.

items.text: IT funding models support diverse investment types including innovation, maintenance, and growth.

rubric.initial: A single undifferentiated funding pool is used for all IT spending with no categorization.
rubric.basic: Basic funding categories exist but allocation across investment types is not strategically managed.
rubric.intermediate: Distinct funding streams are defined for different investment types with documented allocation criteria.
rubric.advanced: Portfolio-based funding models balance run, grow, and transform investments with dynamic rebalancing.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive funding models use real-time value signals to continuously optimize investment mix across categories.

items.text: Investment prioritization uses consistent criteria that balance financial and strategic considerations.

rubric.initial: IT investments are prioritized informally based on politics or urgency rather than consistent criteria.
rubric.basic: Some prioritization criteria exist but are applied inconsistently across investment decisions.
rubric.intermediate: A documented scoring model balances financial and strategic criteria for all significant investments.
rubric.advanced: Multi-criteria decision analysis integrates quantitative and qualitative factors with sensitivity analysis.
rubric.optimizing: AI-assisted prioritization engines optimize the investment portfolio against strategic objectives in real time.

items.text: Business cases for IT investments include comprehensive financial analysis and risk assessment.

rubric.initial: Business cases are not required or are superficial when produced.
rubric.basic: Business cases exist for major investments but financial analysis is limited to basic cost estimates.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized business case templates include NPV, IRR, payback period, and qualitative risk assessment.
rubric.advanced: Business cases use Monte Carlo simulation and scenario analysis with post-implementation validation.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous business case refinement uses actual performance data to improve future investment accuracy.

items.text: Stage-gate funding mechanisms control investment progression based on demonstrated value delivery.

rubric.initial: Investments receive full funding upfront with no interim checkpoints or gates.
rubric.basic: Informal checkpoints exist but rarely result in funding changes or project termination.
rubric.intermediate: Formal stage-gates with defined criteria control funding release at key milestones.
rubric.advanced: Data-driven gates use earned value metrics and value delivery evidence to inform funding decisions.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous funding models adjust investment levels dynamically based on real-time value delivery signals.

items.text: Alternative funding mechanisms such as innovation funds and venture-style investments are utilized.

rubric.initial: All IT funding comes through traditional annual budget processes with no alternative mechanisms.
rubric.basic: Occasional special funding requests are made but no formal alternative funding mechanisms exist.
rubric.intermediate: Dedicated innovation funds or similar mechanisms are established with documented governance processes.
rubric.advanced: A portfolio of funding mechanisms including innovation funds, venture investments, and crowdsourcing is managed.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive funding ecosystems dynamically allocate resources across traditional and alternative channels based on value signals.

items.text: Long-term IT financial sustainability is planned for, considering technology lifecycle costs.

rubric.initial: No long-term financial planning for IT exists; budgets are purely annual.
rubric.basic: Some multi-year cost projections exist but are limited to known commitments.
rubric.intermediate: Multi-year financial plans account for technology refresh cycles, licensing renewals, and growth projections.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive lifecycle costing models inform long-term financial strategy with scenario planning.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic financial sustainability models continuously adjust based on technology trends and business evolution.

items.text: Cloud and subscription-based financing models are managed to optimize cost efficiency.

rubric.initial: Cloud and subscription costs are unmanaged and growing without visibility or control.
rubric.basic: Basic tracking of cloud/subscription costs exists but optimization efforts are minimal.
rubric.intermediate: FinOps practices are established with regular right-sizing, reserved instance planning, and cost tagging.
rubric.advanced: Automated FinOps tools continuously optimize cloud spending with showback/chargeback to business units.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven cloud financial management predicts demand patterns and autonomously optimizes commitments.

items.text: Vendor financial arrangements are structured to maximize value and minimize financial risk.

rubric.initial: Vendor contracts are accepted as offered with no financial negotiation or risk mitigation.
rubric.basic: Some negotiation occurs for large contracts but financial terms are not strategically optimized.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized negotiation frameworks optimize payment terms, volume discounts, and risk-sharing clauses.
rubric.advanced: Strategic vendor financial management includes gainsharing, outcome-based pricing, and financial risk hedging.
rubric.optimizing: Portfolio-level vendor financial optimization uses market intelligence and predictive analytics for maximum value.

items.text: IT investments are tracked through their complete lifecycle from initiation to retirement.

rubric.initial: No lifecycle tracking exists; investments are funded and then forgotten.
rubric.basic: Active projects are tracked but post-implementation and retirement phases are not managed.
rubric.intermediate: A complete investment lifecycle process tracks initiatives from ideation through benefits realization to retirement.
rubric.advanced: Lifecycle management integrates with financial planning and portfolio tools for end-to-end visibility.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive lifecycle models anticipate transition points and proactively trigger planning activities.

items.text: Sunset and decommissioning costs are planned for and included in total investment analysis.

rubric.initial: Sunset and decommissioning costs are never considered in investment planning.
rubric.basic: Decommissioning costs are acknowledged but not quantified or included in financial analysis.
rubric.intermediate: Sunset costs are estimated and included in business cases and lifecycle cost projections.
rubric.advanced: Decommissioning plans with detailed cost estimates are maintained and updated throughout the asset lifecycle.
rubric.optimizing: Automated lifecycle models predict optimal retirement timing and costs based on usage and technology trends.

items.text: Investment performance is reviewed at defined lifecycle stages to confirm continued funding justification.

rubric.initial: No post-approval reviews of investment performance occur.
rubric.basic: Occasional reviews happen for troubled projects but healthy investments are not reassessed.
rubric.intermediate: Mandatory lifecycle reviews at defined stages assess performance and confirm continued investment justification.
rubric.advanced: Continuous performance monitoring with automated triggers initiates reviews when metrics deviate from plans.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven lifecycle analysis continuously evaluates investment health and recommends optimal funding adjustments.

items.text: Lessons from completed investments inform future funding decisions and improve estimation accuracy.

rubric.initial: No lessons are captured from completed investments.
rubric.basic: Post-project reviews occasionally occur but findings are not systematically applied to future decisions.
rubric.intermediate: Structured post-investment reviews capture lessons that feed into updated estimation models and decision criteria.
rubric.advanced: A comprehensive investment knowledge base enables pattern-based estimation and risk-informed funding decisions.
rubric.optimizing: Machine learning continuously improves funding models by analyzing outcomes across the full investment portfolio.

items.text: Financial models are stress-tested against various economic and business scenarios.

rubric.initial: Financial models are not stress-tested; a single scenario is assumed.
rubric.basic: Occasional what-if analysis is performed for major investments.
rubric.intermediate: Systematic scenario analysis tests financial models against defined economic and business scenarios.
rubric.advanced: Monte Carlo simulation and sensitivity analysis provide probabilistic financial projections.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous scenario monitoring automatically updates financial projections based on real-world condition changes.

items.text: IT finance partners with enterprise finance to ensure integrated financial planning and reporting.

rubric.initial: IT and enterprise finance operate independently with no collaboration.
rubric.basic: Periodic data exchange occurs but financial planning is not integrated.
rubric.intermediate: Regular collaboration between IT and enterprise finance ensures aligned planning and consistent reporting.
rubric.advanced: Embedded finance partners provide dedicated IT financial management with dual reporting lines.
rubric.optimizing: Seamless IT-enterprise finance integration provides unified financial intelligence across the organization.

items.text: External financial partnerships including venture capital and co-investment models are explored for strategic IT initiatives.

rubric.initial: All IT funding comes from internal sources with no external partnerships.
rubric.basic: Awareness of external funding options exists but no partnerships have been established.
rubric.intermediate: Selected strategic initiatives leverage external financial partnerships with documented governance.
rubric.advanced: A portfolio of external financial partnerships supports innovation with structured co-investment models.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic financial ecosystems optimize the mix of internal and external funding sources for maximum strategic value.

items.text: Financial performance incentives align IT and business interests in value delivery.

rubric.initial: No financial incentives link IT performance to business value delivery.
rubric.basic: Some IT leaders have financial targets but incentives are not broadly aligned with business outcomes.
rubric.intermediate: Structured incentive programs link IT financial performance to business value metrics.
rubric.advanced: Shared incentive models align IT and business leaders around common value delivery targets.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic incentive systems adapt to strategic priorities and continuously reinforce value-aligned behaviors.

items.text: The organization conducts regular capability maturity assessments across IT functions.

rubric.initial: No formal capability assessments are performed. Maturity levels are unknown.
rubric.basic: Ad hoc assessments are conducted for some IT functions, but there is no consistent methodology.
rubric.intermediate: A standardized maturity assessment framework is used across IT functions on a regular schedule.
rubric.advanced: Maturity assessments are integrated into planning cycles, with trend analysis and cross-functional benchmarking.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous, automated maturity tracking feeds predictive models that anticipate capability gaps before they impact delivery.

items.text: Assessment results are used to drive capability improvement initiatives.

rubric.initial: Assessment results, where they exist, are not systematically used for improvement planning.
rubric.basic: Some assessment findings lead to improvement actions, but follow-through is inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Documented improvement plans are created from assessment results and tracked to completion.
rubric.advanced: Improvement initiatives are prioritized using assessment data, with measurable outcomes and executive sponsorship.
rubric.optimizing: A closed-loop system connects assessments to improvement programs, with real-time progress tracking and adaptive prioritization.

items.text: Assessment processes include input from multiple stakeholder groups including business partners.

rubric.initial: Assessments, if performed, are conducted in isolation by IT without business input.
rubric.basic: Some business stakeholders provide informal input to assessments on an ad hoc basis.
rubric.intermediate: A defined set of stakeholders from IT and business units participate in assessments through structured processes.
rubric.advanced: Multi-stakeholder assessment panels are standard, with balanced representation and calibrated scoring methods.
rubric.optimizing: Stakeholder participation is optimized through dynamic engagement models that ensure comprehensive, unbiased capability evaluation.

items.text: IT capabilities are benchmarked against industry peers and recognized standards.

rubric.initial: No benchmarking is performed. The organization has no visibility into how its capabilities compare externally.
rubric.basic: Informal comparisons are made with peers, but no structured benchmarking program exists.
rubric.intermediate: Formal benchmarking is conducted periodically using recognized frameworks and peer data.
rubric.advanced: Benchmarking is systematic, with curated peer groups and detailed gap analysis informing strategic decisions.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous benchmarking leverages real-time industry data and predictive analytics to maintain competitive positioning.

items.text: Gap analysis outputs are translated into actionable roadmaps with clear ownership.

rubric.initial: No gap analysis is performed or results are not acted upon.
rubric.basic: Gaps are identified but remediation plans are informal and lack clear ownership.
rubric.intermediate: Gap analysis produces documented roadmaps with assigned owners and timelines.
rubric.advanced: Roadmaps are integrated with portfolio planning, resourced appropriately, and tracked through governance processes.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic roadmaps auto-adjust based on changing priorities, with AI-assisted resource optimization and proactive gap prevention.

items.text: Capability assessment tools and platforms are standardized and integrated with planning systems.

rubric.initial: No assessment tools exist; assessments are conducted manually if at all.
rubric.basic: Basic survey tools are used but not standardized or integrated with other systems.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized assessment platforms are deployed with documented integration to planning tools.
rubric.advanced: Integrated assessment platforms automate data collection and feed results directly into capability planning.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent assessment platforms adapt questionnaires dynamically and provide real-time capability insights.

items.text: The organization has a scalable analytics infrastructure that supports diverse data workloads.

rubric.initial: Analytics is performed using spreadsheets and manual processes with no dedicated infrastructure.
rubric.basic: Some analytics tools are deployed but infrastructure is fragmented and not centrally managed.
rubric.intermediate: A standardized analytics platform is in place with defined data pipelines and governance.
rubric.advanced: A scalable, cloud-enabled analytics infrastructure supports real-time and batch processing with automated data quality controls.
rubric.optimizing: Self-optimizing analytics infrastructure dynamically scales resources, with automated performance tuning and cost optimization.

items.text: Data governance policies ensure data quality, lineage, and appropriate access controls for analytics.

rubric.initial: No formal data governance exists. Data quality is unknown and access is uncontrolled.
rubric.basic: Basic data governance policies exist but are inconsistently applied across the organization.
rubric.intermediate: Data governance is formalized with defined roles, quality standards, lineage tracking, and access policies.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive data governance integrates automated quality monitoring, full lineage tracing, and role-based access management.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive governance leverages ML-based data quality detection, automated classification, and self-healing data pipelines.

items.text: A data catalog is maintained to enable discovery and understanding of organizational data assets.

rubric.initial: No data catalog exists. Users rely on tribal knowledge to find and understand data.
rubric.basic: A partial inventory of key data sources exists but is not actively maintained.
rubric.intermediate: A searchable data catalog is maintained with metadata, descriptions, and ownership information.
rubric.advanced: The data catalog is integrated with analytics tools, includes usage metrics, and is actively curated by data stewards.
rubric.optimizing: An AI-augmented catalog auto-discovers datasets, infers relationships, recommends relevant data, and tracks usage patterns.

items.text: Data science capabilities are established to deliver predictive and prescriptive insights.

rubric.initial: No data science capability exists. Analysis is limited to historical reporting.
rubric.basic: Individual analysts perform ad hoc predictive analysis, but there is no formal data science function.
rubric.intermediate: A data science team operates with defined methodologies, model lifecycle management, and stakeholder engagement processes.
rubric.advanced: Data science is embedded across business functions with MLOps practices, model monitoring, and automated retraining pipelines.
rubric.optimizing: Advanced AI/ML capabilities deliver autonomous decision support with explainable models, continuous learning, and ethical AI governance.

items.text: Self-service analytics tools empower business users to explore data and generate insights independently.

rubric.initial: All reporting and analysis requests go through IT with long turnaround times.
rubric.basic: Some self-service tools are available but adoption is low and support is limited.
rubric.intermediate: Self-service analytics platforms are deployed with training programs and curated datasets for business users.
rubric.advanced: Widespread self-service adoption with governed data marts, automated insights, and community-driven best practices.
rubric.optimizing: Natural language query interfaces and AI-driven insight generation enable all employees to perform sophisticated analysis without technical skills.

items.text: Data analytics skills and literacy are developed across the organization to enable data-driven culture.

rubric.initial: No data analytics training or literacy programs exist.
rubric.basic: Ad hoc training is available for some analysts but organizational literacy is low.
rubric.intermediate: Structured data literacy programs build analytics skills across business and IT functions.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive analytics academies develop advanced skills with role-based curricula and certification paths.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive learning platforms personalize analytics skill development based on role needs and emerging techniques.

items.text: An enterprise architecture framework is adopted and consistently applied across the organization.

rubric.initial: No enterprise architecture framework exists. Technology decisions are made in silos.
rubric.basic: An architecture framework has been selected but adoption is limited to specific projects or teams.
rubric.intermediate: The EA framework is consistently applied with documented standards, review processes, and exception management.
rubric.advanced: The EA framework drives all technology decisions, with automated compliance checking and integration into project governance.
rubric.optimizing: The architecture framework continuously evolves through industry engagement, with AI-assisted pattern recognition and proactive standards evolution.

items.text: Architecture standards and reference models guide technology selection and solution design.

rubric.initial: No architecture standards exist. Technology choices are inconsistent and duplicative.
rubric.basic: Some technology standards are documented but enforcement is weak and exceptions are common.
rubric.intermediate: Comprehensive reference architectures and standards are published, with formal review and waiver processes.
rubric.advanced: Standards are integrated into development toolchains with automated conformance validation and impact analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Living reference models adapt based on emerging technologies, usage patterns, and business outcome data.

items.text: Architecture artifacts are maintained in a central repository accessible to all stakeholders.

rubric.initial: Architecture documentation is scattered, outdated, or nonexistent.
rubric.basic: Some architecture diagrams exist but are maintained informally and are difficult to find.
rubric.intermediate: A central EA repository contains current models, diagrams, and standards with defined update processes.
rubric.advanced: The repository is integrated with CMDB and project tools, providing real-time visibility into the current and target architecture.
rubric.optimizing: An intelligent architecture repository auto-updates from infrastructure telemetry and provides simulation capabilities for change impact analysis.

items.text: Technology roadmaps are defined, maintained, and aligned with business strategy.

rubric.initial: No technology roadmaps exist. Technology evolution is unplanned.
rubric.basic: Informal technology roadmaps exist for some domains but are not aligned with business strategy.
rubric.intermediate: Formal roadmaps cover all major technology domains and are reviewed quarterly with business stakeholders.
rubric.advanced: Roadmaps are dynamically maintained, linked to business outcomes, and inform investment decisions across the portfolio.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive roadmapping uses market intelligence and business scenario modeling to anticipate technology needs before business demand.

items.text: Architecture governance ensures solution designs comply with enterprise standards and principles.

rubric.initial: No architecture governance exists. Solutions are designed without reference to enterprise standards.
rubric.basic: Informal architecture reviews occur for large projects but there is no consistent governance process.
rubric.intermediate: A formal architecture review board evaluates designs against standards with documented decisions and exceptions.
rubric.advanced: Governance is embedded in the delivery lifecycle with automated compliance gates, risk scoring, and remediation tracking.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent governance provides real-time design guidance, automated conformance checking, and predictive technical debt analysis.

items.text: Enterprise architecture governance ensures technology decisions align with architectural principles.

rubric.initial: No architecture governance exists; technology decisions are made without architectural review.
rubric.basic: Some architecture review occurs for major decisions but governance is informal.
rubric.intermediate: Architecture review boards evaluate significant technology decisions against documented principles and standards.
rubric.advanced: Integrated architecture governance is embedded in procurement, project, and change management processes.
rubric.optimizing: Automated architecture compliance checking provides real-time guidance for technology decisions.

items.text: A comprehensive information security strategy and policy framework is established and maintained.

rubric.initial: No formal security strategy or policies exist. Security measures are ad hoc and reactive.
rubric.basic: Basic security policies exist but are incomplete, outdated, or inconsistently enforced.
rubric.intermediate: A comprehensive security policy framework aligned to industry standards (e.g., ISO 27001, NIST) is maintained and regularly reviewed.
rubric.advanced: Security strategy is integrated with business risk management, with automated policy enforcement and compliance monitoring.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive security strategy leverages threat intelligence and business context to proactively evolve policies ahead of emerging risks.

items.text: Security risk assessments are conducted regularly across all IT assets and business processes.

rubric.initial: No formal security risk assessments are performed.
rubric.basic: Security risk assessments are conducted reactively, typically after incidents or for compliance audits.
rubric.intermediate: Regular risk assessments cover all critical assets using a standardized methodology with documented risk registers.
rubric.advanced: Continuous risk assessment integrates vulnerability scanning, threat modeling, and business impact analysis across all assets.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven risk assessment provides real-time risk scoring with predictive threat modeling and automated remediation prioritization.

items.text: Security awareness training is provided to all employees and effectiveness is measured.

rubric.initial: No security awareness training program exists.
rubric.basic: Annual security awareness training is provided but completion tracking and effectiveness measurement are limited.
rubric.intermediate: Role-based security training is delivered regularly with completion tracking and knowledge assessments.
rubric.advanced: Continuous security awareness programs include simulated attacks, gamification, and behavioral metrics to measure culture change.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive training uses AI to personalize content based on individual risk profiles, role changes, and emerging threat patterns.

items.text: Threat detection and monitoring capabilities provide visibility across the technology environment.

rubric.initial: No centralized threat detection or monitoring capabilities exist.
rubric.basic: Basic logging and alerting are in place for some systems but visibility is fragmented.
rubric.intermediate: A SIEM platform aggregates logs across the environment with defined detection rules and alert triage processes.
rubric.advanced: Advanced threat detection combines SIEM, EDR, and network analytics with automated correlation and threat hunting capabilities.
rubric.optimizing: AI-powered security operations provide autonomous threat detection, behavioral analysis, and predictive threat intelligence across all attack surfaces.

items.text: Incident response processes are defined, tested, and enable rapid containment and recovery.

rubric.initial: No formal incident response plan exists. Responses are ad hoc and uncoordinated.
rubric.basic: A basic incident response plan exists but has not been tested and roles are unclear.
rubric.intermediate: Documented IR procedures are tested through tabletop exercises with defined roles, escalation paths, and communication plans.
rubric.advanced: Automated incident response playbooks enable rapid containment, with integrated forensics and lessons-learned processes.
rubric.optimizing: Self-healing security orchestration autonomously contains threats, with AI-assisted forensics and continuous response optimization.

items.text: Security operations include advanced threat detection and automated incident response capabilities.

rubric.initial: No security operations center or advanced threat detection capabilities exist.
rubric.basic: Basic security monitoring exists but threat detection relies on signature-based tools only.
rubric.intermediate: A security operations center uses behavioral analytics and threat intelligence for detection and response.
rubric.advanced: Advanced SOC capabilities include automated incident response, threat hunting, and orchestration platforms.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven security operations predict threats, autonomously respond to incidents, and continuously adapt defenses.

items.text: Processes exist to capture, organize, and maintain institutional knowledge across IT.

rubric.initial: Knowledge resides in individuals' heads. No systematic capture or documentation processes exist.
rubric.basic: Some documentation exists in wikis or shared drives, but it is inconsistent and difficult to find.
rubric.intermediate: A centralized knowledge management system is used with defined contribution workflows, taxonomies, and quality standards.
rubric.advanced: Knowledge capture is embedded in work processes with automated tagging, quality reviews, and expiration management.
rubric.optimizing: AI-powered knowledge systems automatically capture knowledge from interactions, code, and tickets, keeping content current without manual effort.

items.text: Critical knowledge areas are identified and protected against key-person dependency risks.

rubric.initial: Key-person dependencies are unknown. Knowledge loss from departures is a recurring problem.
rubric.basic: Some critical knowledge areas are informally recognized but no systematic risk mitigation is in place.
rubric.intermediate: Critical knowledge domains are mapped, key-person risks are assessed, and cross-training plans are documented.
rubric.advanced: Knowledge risk management is integrated with workforce planning, with automated dependency detection and proactive knowledge transfer programs.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive analytics identify emerging knowledge risks and trigger automated knowledge capture and redistribution before gaps materialize.

items.text: Knowledge articles are reviewed and updated regularly to ensure accuracy and relevance.

rubric.initial: No review processes exist. Documentation quickly becomes outdated and unreliable.
rubric.basic: Occasional reviews happen but there is no systematic schedule or ownership for content maintenance.
rubric.intermediate: Defined review cycles with assigned owners ensure knowledge articles are periodically validated and updated.
rubric.advanced: Usage analytics and feedback mechanisms prioritize reviews, with automated staleness detection and content health dashboards.
rubric.optimizing: Self-maintaining knowledge bases use AI to detect outdated content, suggest updates, and validate accuracy against current systems.

items.text: A culture of knowledge sharing is fostered through tools, incentives, and communities of practice.

rubric.initial: Knowledge hoarding is common. There are no incentives or mechanisms for sharing.
rubric.basic: Some knowledge sharing happens informally, but there are no structured communities or incentive programs.
rubric.intermediate: Communities of practice are established with regular forums, and knowledge sharing is recognized in performance processes.
rubric.advanced: Knowledge sharing is embedded in organizational culture with gamification, contribution metrics, and executive sponsorship.
rubric.optimizing: AI-facilitated knowledge networks proactively connect experts with those who need help, measuring and optimizing knowledge flow across the organization.

items.text: Lessons learned from projects and incidents are systematically captured and applied to future work.

rubric.initial: No lessons-learned process exists. The same mistakes are repeated across projects and incidents.
rubric.basic: Lessons learned are occasionally documented but rarely referenced or applied in future work.
rubric.intermediate: Structured retrospectives and post-incident reviews produce documented lessons that are indexed and searchable.
rubric.advanced: Lessons learned are automatically surfaced during planning and design phases, with traceability to implemented improvements.
rubric.optimizing: Pattern recognition across historical lessons provides predictive guidance, automatically flagging risks and recommending proven approaches for new initiatives.

items.text: Communities of practice foster knowledge sharing and collaborative problem-solving across IT.

rubric.initial: No communities of practice or knowledge-sharing forums exist.
rubric.basic: Informal knowledge sharing occurs but communities of practice are not formally supported.
rubric.intermediate: Sponsored communities of practice operate with defined charters, facilitators, and participation expectations.
rubric.advanced: Vibrant communities drive cross-functional innovation with executive support and measurable outcomes.
rubric.optimizing: Self-organizing knowledge networks dynamically form around emerging topics with AI-curated content.

items.text: IT workforce planning identifies current and future skill needs aligned with technology strategy.

rubric.initial: No workforce planning exists. Hiring is reactive and driven by immediate vacancies.
rubric.basic: Basic headcount planning exists but is not informed by technology strategy or skills gap analysis.
rubric.intermediate: Workforce planning includes skills inventories, gap analysis, and alignment with the technology roadmap.
rubric.advanced: Strategic workforce planning integrates market intelligence, succession planning, and build-buy-borrow strategies for critical skills.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive workforce analytics anticipate skill needs based on technology trends and business scenarios, enabling proactive talent pipeline development.

items.text: Career development paths and continuous learning programs are available for IT staff.

rubric.initial: No formal career paths or learning programs exist for IT roles.
rubric.basic: Generic training budgets exist but there are no defined career paths or structured development programs.
rubric.intermediate: Role-based career paths and development programs are documented with individual development plans for all IT staff.
rubric.advanced: Personalized learning journeys combine formal training, mentoring, rotations, and stretch assignments with skills-based progression.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven development platforms recommend personalized learning paths based on career aspirations, market demand, and organizational needs.

items.text: Recruitment processes attract diverse, high-quality IT talent in a competitive market.

rubric.initial: Recruitment is slow and ad hoc with no defined employer brand for IT roles.
rubric.basic: Standard recruitment processes exist but time-to-hire is long and candidate quality is inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Optimized recruitment includes employer branding, diverse sourcing channels, structured interviews, and candidate experience management.
rubric.advanced: Data-driven recruitment uses analytics to optimize sourcing, assess cultural fit, and reduce bias while maintaining speed and quality.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive talent intelligence identifies and engages passive candidates before needs arise, with continuous optimization of hiring outcomes.

items.text: Retention strategies address key drivers of IT employee engagement and satisfaction.

rubric.initial: No formal retention strategies exist. Turnover causes are unknown.
rubric.basic: Exit interviews are conducted but retention actions are reactive and not data-driven.
rubric.intermediate: Regular engagement surveys inform targeted retention programs addressing compensation, growth, and work environment.
rubric.advanced: Predictive attrition models identify at-risk employees, enabling proactive intervention with personalized retention strategies.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous sentiment analysis and career satisfaction monitoring drive real-time, personalized engagement actions that maintain industry-leading retention rates.

items.text: Succession plans are in place for critical IT leadership and specialist roles.

rubric.initial: No succession planning exists. Leadership transitions are disruptive and unplanned.
rubric.basic: Informal successors are identified for some roles but development plans are not in place.
rubric.intermediate: Formal succession plans exist for critical roles with identified successors and documented development paths.
rubric.advanced: Succession planning is integrated with talent development, with ready-now and pipeline candidates actively developed and assessed.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic succession models continuously evaluate readiness, simulate transition scenarios, and automatically trigger development actions for pipeline gaps.

items.text: Workforce planning anticipates future skill needs driven by technology trends and business strategy.

rubric.initial: No workforce planning for future skill needs is conducted.
rubric.basic: Some awareness of future skill gaps exists but planning is reactive.
rubric.intermediate: Structured workforce planning identifies future skill needs based on technology roadmaps and strategy.
rubric.advanced: Predictive workforce models quantify future skill demands with sourcing strategies for critical capabilities.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic workforce planning continuously adapts to emerging skill requirements using market intelligence.

items.text: A comprehensive privacy framework governs the collection, processing, and storage of personal data.

rubric.initial: No privacy framework exists. Personal data handling is uncontrolled and undocumented.
rubric.basic: Basic privacy policies exist but are incomplete and not consistently applied across the organization.
rubric.intermediate: A comprehensive privacy framework aligned to applicable regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA) is implemented with defined roles and processes.
rubric.advanced: Privacy by design is embedded in all systems and processes, with automated data mapping and privacy impact assessments.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive privacy management uses AI to detect new data processing activities, auto-classify data, and maintain continuous regulatory alignment.

items.text: Data protection controls ensure personal data is secured throughout its lifecycle.

rubric.initial: No specific controls protect personal data. Encryption and access controls are absent or inconsistent.
rubric.basic: Basic encryption and access controls exist for some personal data stores but coverage is incomplete.
rubric.intermediate: Comprehensive data protection controls including encryption, anonymization, and access management are applied to all personal data.
rubric.advanced: Automated data protection enforcement ensures consistent controls across all environments with continuous monitoring and anomaly detection.
rubric.optimizing: Zero-trust data protection with dynamic, context-aware controls automatically adapts protection levels based on data sensitivity and usage patterns.

items.text: Data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability) are fulfilled efficiently.

rubric.initial: No process exists to handle data subject requests. The organization cannot locate or manage personal data on request.
rubric.basic: Data subject requests are handled manually on a case-by-case basis with long turnaround times.
rubric.intermediate: Documented processes with defined SLAs handle data subject requests, supported by data discovery tools.
rubric.advanced: Automated workflows process data subject requests across all systems with audit trails and compliance reporting.
rubric.optimizing: Self-service portals enable data subjects to exercise rights directly, with automated fulfillment and real-time compliance dashboards.

items.text: Privacy compliance is monitored and maintained across all applicable jurisdictions and regulations.

rubric.initial: No awareness of applicable privacy regulations. Compliance status is unknown.
rubric.basic: Key regulations are identified but compliance monitoring is manual and infrequent.
rubric.intermediate: A regulatory compliance program tracks obligations across jurisdictions with regular assessments and gap remediation.
rubric.advanced: Automated compliance monitoring provides real-time visibility into compliance status with proactive alerting on regulatory changes.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive regulatory intelligence anticipates new requirements and automatically adjusts controls and processes before enforcement deadlines.

items.text: Privacy impact assessments are conducted for new initiatives involving personal data.

rubric.initial: No privacy impact assessments are performed for new projects or systems.
rubric.basic: Privacy assessments are conducted for major projects but are inconsistent and lack a standard methodology.
rubric.intermediate: Mandatory privacy impact assessments use a standardized methodology and are integrated into the project approval process.
rubric.advanced: Automated PIA workflows with risk scoring are embedded in delivery pipelines, with continuous monitoring post-implementation.
rubric.optimizing: AI-assisted privacy assessments predict risks from design patterns, recommend mitigations, and continuously validate privacy controls in production.

items.text: Data subject rights processes are automated and meet regulatory response timelines.

rubric.initial: No processes exist to handle data subject rights requests.
rubric.basic: Manual processes handle rights requests but responses often miss regulatory deadlines.
rubric.intermediate: Documented processes handle data subject requests within regulatory timelines with tracking and reporting.
rubric.advanced: Automated workflows manage rights requests end-to-end with self-service portals and compliance dashboards.
rubric.optimizing: AI-powered systems proactively manage data subject preferences and anticipate regulatory requirement changes.

items.text: Programme governance structures provide oversight, decision-making authority, and escalation paths.

rubric.initial: No programme governance exists. Large initiatives are managed as disconnected projects without coordination.
rubric.basic: Informal governance exists for some programmes but roles, responsibilities, and decision rights are unclear.
rubric.intermediate: Formal programme governance structures are defined with clear roles, stage gates, and escalation procedures.
rubric.advanced: Governance is risk-adaptive, with automated reporting, real-time health dashboards, and integrated portfolio-level decision support.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent governance leverages predictive analytics to anticipate programme risks and recommend proactive interventions before issues escalate.

items.text: Programme managers have the skills and authority to manage complex, cross-functional initiatives.

rubric.initial: No dedicated programme management capability exists. Complex initiatives lack coordinated leadership.
rubric.basic: Some programme managers are in place but lack formal training, tools, or sufficient authority.
rubric.intermediate: Qualified programme managers lead initiatives with defined competency frameworks, tools, and organizational authority.
rubric.advanced: A mature programme management office develops talent, shares best practices, and provides consistent methodology and tooling.
rubric.optimizing: Programme management excellence is a strategic differentiator, with advanced simulation, AI-assisted planning, and continuous methodology refinement.

items.text: Programme benefits are defined, tracked, and realized through structured benefits management.

rubric.initial: Benefits are not defined or tracked. There is no visibility into whether programmes deliver expected value.
rubric.basic: Benefits are stated in business cases but are not systematically tracked post-approval.
rubric.intermediate: A benefits realization framework tracks defined benefits through the programme lifecycle with regular reviews.
rubric.advanced: Benefits tracking is integrated with financial systems, with variance analysis and corrective action processes for at-risk benefits.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive benefits modeling forecasts realization probability in real time, enabling dynamic programme adjustments to maximize value delivery.

items.text: Interdependencies between projects within programmes are identified, managed, and communicated.

rubric.initial: Interdependencies between projects are not identified. Conflicts and delays occur frequently due to unmanaged dependencies.
rubric.basic: Some dependencies are known but management is informal and issues are resolved reactively.
rubric.intermediate: Dependency mapping is performed and maintained, with regular reviews and documented mitigation plans.
rubric.advanced: Automated dependency tracking integrates with project schedules, providing real-time impact analysis and proactive conflict resolution.
rubric.optimizing: AI-powered dependency management predicts cascading impacts, simulates schedule scenarios, and recommends optimal sequencing across the portfolio.

items.text: Stakeholder communication within programmes is structured, timely, and role-appropriate.

rubric.initial: No structured programme communications exist. Stakeholders are uninformed or receive inconsistent information.
rubric.basic: Ad hoc status updates are provided, but communication frequency and content vary widely.
rubric.intermediate: A programme communication plan defines audiences, channels, frequency, and content for all stakeholder groups.
rubric.advanced: Automated, role-based communication delivers personalized dashboards, alerts, and reports tailored to each stakeholder's needs.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent communication platforms adapt content and timing based on stakeholder engagement patterns and decision-making needs.

items.text: Programme benefits realization is tracked and reported throughout the programme lifecycle.

rubric.initial: Programme benefits are stated at initiation but never tracked or reported.
rubric.basic: Benefits are reviewed at programme closure but not tracked during execution.
rubric.intermediate: Benefits tracking is integrated into programme reporting with regular reviews against targets.
rubric.advanced: Real-time benefits dashboards show realization progress with predictive forecasting.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive benefits management continuously refines targets and accelerates realization based on delivery data.

items.text: A defined project management methodology is consistently applied across IT projects.

rubric.initial: No standard project management methodology exists. Each project is run differently.
rubric.basic: A project methodology has been selected but adoption is inconsistent across teams and projects.
rubric.intermediate: A standardized methodology (waterfall, agile, or hybrid) is consistently applied with defined processes, templates, and tools.
rubric.advanced: Methodology is adaptive, with project managers selecting and tailoring approaches based on project characteristics and risk profiles.
rubric.optimizing: AI-assisted project management recommends optimal methodologies, predicts risks, and continuously improves processes based on outcome data.

items.text: Project scope, schedule, and budget are effectively managed with appropriate change controls.

rubric.initial: Projects frequently exceed budget and timelines. No change control processes exist.
rubric.basic: Basic tracking of scope, schedule, and budget exists but change management is informal.
rubric.intermediate: Formal change control processes manage scope, schedule, and budget with documented approvals and impact assessments.
rubric.advanced: Integrated project controls provide real-time performance data with earned value management and automated variance alerting.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive project analytics forecast completion dates and costs with high accuracy, enabling proactive corrective actions.

items.text: Project risks are systematically identified, assessed, and managed throughout the project lifecycle.

rubric.initial: Project risks are not formally identified or managed. Issues are addressed only when they occur.
rubric.basic: Risk registers exist for some projects but are not actively maintained or used for decision-making.
rubric.intermediate: Risk management is embedded in the project lifecycle with regular risk reviews, quantified impact assessments, and mitigation plans.
rubric.advanced: Advanced risk management includes Monte Carlo simulations, quantitative risk analysis, and integrated risk-response tracking.
rubric.optimizing: Machine learning models identify risk patterns from historical projects, providing early warning indicators and recommended mitigation strategies.

items.text: Project quality management ensures deliverables meet defined standards and stakeholder expectations.

rubric.initial: No quality management processes exist for projects. Deliverable quality is unpredictable.
rubric.basic: Basic quality checks occur at project completion but there are no defined quality standards or in-process reviews.
rubric.intermediate: Quality management plans define standards, review gates, and acceptance criteria for all projects.
rubric.advanced: Continuous quality monitoring with automated testing, peer reviews, and quality metrics drives improvement throughout the project lifecycle.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive quality analytics identify defect patterns early, enabling prevention-focused quality management that consistently exceeds stakeholder expectations.

items.text: Stakeholder engagement is actively managed to ensure alignment and support throughout projects.

rubric.initial: Stakeholder management is not practiced. Misalignment and resistance are common.
rubric.basic: Key stakeholders are identified but engagement is reactive and communication is inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Stakeholder management plans identify stakeholders, assess influence, and define engagement strategies for each project.
rubric.advanced: Stakeholder engagement is measured through satisfaction surveys and sentiment analysis, with adaptive engagement strategies.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven stakeholder analytics predict engagement risks and recommend personalized communication strategies to maintain alignment.

items.text: Project management practices are tailored based on project type, size, and risk profile.

rubric.initial: A one-size-fits-all project management approach is used regardless of project characteristics.
rubric.basic: Some variation in approach exists but tailoring is informal and inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Documented project categorization determines appropriate methodology, governance, and reporting requirements.
rubric.advanced: Adaptive project frameworks dynamically adjust practices based on project context and emerging conditions.
rubric.optimizing: AI-assisted project setup recommends optimal methodology, team structure, and governance based on project attributes.

items.text: Dedicated business relationship managers bridge the gap between IT and business units.

rubric.initial: No formal relationship management exists between IT and business units. Interactions are transactional and reactive.
rubric.basic: Some IT staff liaise with business units informally, but there are no dedicated BRM roles or processes.
rubric.intermediate: Dedicated BRMs are assigned to business units with defined engagement models, regular touchpoints, and documented outcomes.
rubric.advanced: BRMs are strategic advisors embedded in business planning processes, influencing demand shaping and digital strategy.
rubric.optimizing: BRMs operate as co-creators of business value, using advanced analytics to proactively identify opportunities and anticipate business needs.

items.text: IT-business alignment is measured and improved through structured feedback mechanisms.

rubric.initial: No mechanisms exist to measure IT-business alignment. Perceptions of IT value are anecdotal.
rubric.basic: Occasional surveys capture business satisfaction with IT, but results are not systematically acted upon.
rubric.intermediate: Regular satisfaction surveys and feedback channels measure alignment, with improvement plans tracked to completion.
rubric.advanced: Multi-dimensional alignment metrics combine satisfaction data, value delivery measures, and strategic contribution indicators.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time alignment dashboards with sentiment analysis and predictive modeling drive continuous optimization of the IT-business relationship.

items.text: Communication channels between IT and stakeholders are effective, transparent, and multi-directional.

rubric.initial: Communication from IT is rare, technical, and one-directional. Stakeholders feel uninformed.
rubric.basic: Regular IT updates are provided but communication is primarily one-way and not tailored to audiences.
rubric.intermediate: Multi-channel communication strategies deliver targeted, business-relevant information with feedback mechanisms.
rubric.advanced: Interactive platforms enable two-way dialogue, with personalized communications and real-time issue resolution.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent communication systems adapt content, timing, and channels based on individual stakeholder preferences and engagement analytics.

items.text: Stakeholder expectations are actively managed through transparent service commitments and reporting.

rubric.initial: Stakeholder expectations are unmanaged. Frequent disappointments erode trust in IT.
rubric.basic: Informal commitments are made but follow-through is inconsistent and reporting is limited.
rubric.intermediate: Formal service commitments are documented and tracked, with regular performance reporting to stakeholders.
rubric.advanced: Proactive expectation management includes scenario-based planning, early warning communications, and collaborative prioritization.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive stakeholder analytics anticipate expectation shifts and trigger proactive adjustments to commitments and communications.

items.text: Vendor and partner relationships are managed to maximize value and minimize risk.

rubric.initial: Vendor relationships are transactional with no strategic management or performance oversight.
rubric.basic: Key vendor contacts are maintained but relationship management is reactive and contract-focused.
rubric.intermediate: A vendor management framework defines engagement tiers, performance metrics, and regular review processes.
rubric.advanced: Strategic vendor partnerships are actively managed with joint innovation programs, risk sharing, and integrated roadmaps.
rubric.optimizing: AI-augmented vendor intelligence continuously evaluates market alternatives, predicts vendor risks, and optimizes the partner ecosystem.

items.text: Relationship health is measured using quantitative metrics and drives improvement actions.

rubric.initial: Relationship health is not measured; stakeholder sentiment is unknown.
rubric.basic: Informal feedback provides some insight into relationship quality but measurement is absent.
rubric.intermediate: Regular relationship health surveys produce quantitative metrics with documented improvement actions.
rubric.advanced: Multi-dimensional relationship scorecards track trust, satisfaction, and strategic alignment with trend analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time relationship analytics predict relationship risks and recommend proactive engagement strategies.

items.text: A mature software development lifecycle is established with appropriate practices for each phase.

rubric.initial: No defined SDLC exists. Development practices are inconsistent and undocumented.
rubric.basic: A basic SDLC is defined but adherence varies across teams and key phases are often skipped.
rubric.intermediate: A standardized SDLC with defined deliverables, quality gates, and tooling is consistently followed across teams.
rubric.advanced: The SDLC is continuously refined based on metrics, with adaptive practices that scale to different solution types and risk levels.
rubric.optimizing: AI-enhanced development practices optimize the SDLC dynamically, with automated code generation, intelligent testing, and self-improving processes.

items.text: CI/CD pipelines automate build, test, and deployment processes for consistent and rapid delivery.

rubric.initial: Builds, tests, and deployments are manual, error-prone, and time-consuming.
rubric.basic: Some automation exists for builds or deployments but CI/CD is not consistently implemented across teams.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized CI/CD pipelines automate build, test, and deployment with defined quality gates for all applications.
rubric.advanced: Advanced pipelines include automated security scanning, performance testing, canary deployments, and automated rollback capabilities.
rubric.optimizing: Self-optimizing pipelines use ML to predict build failures, optimize test suites, and intelligently manage deployment strategies.

items.text: DevOps culture and practices break down silos between development and operations teams.

rubric.initial: Development and operations work in strict silos with adversarial handoffs and blame culture.
rubric.basic: Some collaboration between dev and ops occurs but organizational silos and distinct processes remain.
rubric.intermediate: Cross-functional teams practice DevOps with shared responsibility for delivery and operations, supported by common tools.
rubric.advanced: Mature DevOps culture with infrastructure as code, SRE practices, blameless postmortems, and end-to-end ownership.
rubric.optimizing: Platform engineering teams provide self-service capabilities, with autonomous teams practicing continuous deployment and full-stack ownership.

items.text: Quality assurance processes ensure solutions meet functional, non-functional, and compliance requirements.

rubric.initial: Testing is ad hoc and minimal. Defects are frequently found in production.
rubric.basic: Basic functional testing is performed but non-functional testing (performance, security) is inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Comprehensive QA processes cover functional, performance, security, and accessibility testing with defined coverage targets.
rubric.advanced: Automated testing frameworks provide continuous quality validation with risk-based test prioritization and defect prediction.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven testing generates test cases, identifies coverage gaps, predicts defect-prone areas, and continuously optimizes the test portfolio.

items.text: Release management processes ensure controlled, low-risk deployments to production.

rubric.initial: Releases are unplanned and high-risk, frequently causing outages and requiring emergency fixes.
rubric.basic: A basic release process exists with some coordination, but deployment failures are still common.
rubric.intermediate: Formal release management includes deployment plans, rollback procedures, change approval, and post-deployment verification.
rubric.advanced: Progressive delivery techniques (blue-green, canary, feature flags) minimize risk with automated health checks and rollback.
rubric.optimizing: Autonomous release orchestration uses real-time telemetry to manage progressive rollouts, with self-healing deployments and zero-downtime releases.

items.text: Solution delivery incorporates DevSecOps practices for integrated security and quality.

rubric.initial: Security is addressed only at the end of delivery or not at all.
rubric.basic: Some security checks occur during delivery but integration with development is minimal.
rubric.intermediate: DevSecOps practices integrate security scanning and testing into CI/CD pipelines.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive DevSecOps includes automated security testing, compliance checking, and threat modeling in delivery.
rubric.optimizing: AI-augmented DevSecOps continuously adapts security practices based on threat intelligence and code patterns.

items.text: IT services are defined in a service catalog with clear descriptions, owners, and support details.

rubric.initial: No service catalog exists. Users are unsure what IT services are available or how to request them.
rubric.basic: A partial service catalog exists but is incomplete, outdated, or difficult for users to access.
rubric.intermediate: A comprehensive service catalog is published and maintained, with clear service descriptions, SLAs, and request processes.
rubric.advanced: A dynamic service catalog integrates with ITSM tools, providing real-time availability, automated provisioning, and usage analytics.
rubric.optimizing: An intelligent service catalog recommends services based on user context, predicts demand, and enables self-service provisioning with automated fulfillment.

items.text: Service level agreements are defined, monitored, and consistently met for all IT services.

rubric.initial: No SLAs are defined. Service performance expectations are unclear and unmanaged.
rubric.basic: SLAs exist for some services but monitoring is manual and breaches are not systematically addressed.
rubric.intermediate: SLAs are defined for all critical services with automated monitoring, regular reporting, and breach management processes.
rubric.advanced: SLA management includes predictive breach alerting, automated remediation triggers, and continuous SLA optimization based on business impact.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven SLA management predicts performance trends, auto-adjusts resource allocation, and proactively prevents breaches before they impact users.

items.text: Service request fulfillment processes are efficient and deliver a positive user experience.

rubric.initial: Service requests are handled through informal channels with no tracking or SLAs.
rubric.basic: A ticketing system captures requests but fulfillment times are long and processes are manual.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized request workflows with defined SLAs provide consistent fulfillment, tracked through a service management platform.
rubric.advanced: Automated fulfillment for common requests, self-service portals, and proactive communication reduce fulfillment times significantly.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent automation handles most requests instantly, with chatbot-assisted triage and predictive provisioning based on user context.

items.text: Incident and problem management processes minimize service disruptions and prevent recurrence.

rubric.initial: No formal incident or problem management processes exist. Issues are resolved ad hoc.
rubric.basic: Basic incident management exists but root cause analysis is rare and recurring issues persist.
rubric.intermediate: Mature incident and problem management with prioritization, escalation, root cause analysis, and known error databases.
rubric.advanced: Automated incident detection, correlation, and initial remediation reduce MTTR, with proactive problem management preventing incidents.
rubric.optimizing: AIOps capabilities predict and prevent incidents, with autonomous remediation, intelligent correlation, and continuous service improvement.

items.text: Continual service improvement processes drive measurable enhancements to service quality.

rubric.initial: No systematic approach to improving IT services exists. Changes happen reactively.
rubric.basic: Improvement ideas are collected informally but there is no structured process for evaluation or implementation.
rubric.intermediate: A CSI register tracks improvement initiatives with defined ownership, prioritization, and progress reporting.
rubric.advanced: Data-driven CSI uses service analytics, user feedback, and benchmark data to identify and prioritize high-impact improvements.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven continuous improvement automatically identifies optimization opportunities, models expected outcomes, and initiates improvement workflows.

items.text: Service provisioning includes self-service capabilities that empower users while maintaining governance.

rubric.initial: All service provisioning requires manual intervention from IT staff.
rubric.basic: Limited self-service exists for simple requests but most provisioning is manual.
rubric.intermediate: Self-service portals enable users to provision standard services with automated approval workflows.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive self-service with automated provisioning, configuration, and compliance checking.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent self-service anticipates user needs and recommends optimal service configurations.

items.text: An infrastructure strategy defines the target state, migration path, and investment priorities.

rubric.initial: No infrastructure strategy exists. Growth and changes are unplanned and reactive.
rubric.basic: An informal infrastructure direction exists but it is not documented or aligned with business needs.
rubric.intermediate: A documented infrastructure strategy covers compute, storage, networking, and cloud with defined migration roadmaps.
rubric.advanced: The strategy is dynamically maintained, aligned with application portfolio needs, and includes total cost of ownership modeling.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive infrastructure planning uses workload analytics and business scenario modeling to optimize investments and timing decisions.

items.text: Infrastructure operations are automated, monitored, and deliver consistent performance.

rubric.initial: Infrastructure management is entirely manual with no monitoring. Outages are detected by users.
rubric.basic: Basic monitoring exists for some systems but operations are largely manual and reactive.
rubric.intermediate: Comprehensive monitoring, alerting, and infrastructure-as-code provide consistent operations with proactive issue detection.
rubric.advanced: Fully automated operations with self-healing capabilities, capacity management, and end-to-end observability across all environments.
rubric.optimizing: AIOps-driven infrastructure autonomously optimizes performance, predicts failures, and self-remediates issues with minimal human intervention.

items.text: Cloud adoption is strategically managed to optimize cost, performance, and agility.

rubric.initial: No cloud strategy exists. Cloud usage, if any, is uncoordinated and unmanaged.
rubric.basic: Some workloads are in the cloud but without a cohesive strategy, governance, or cost management.
rubric.intermediate: A cloud strategy defines workload placement criteria, with governance, security controls, and cost monitoring in place.
rubric.advanced: Multi-cloud management with automated cost optimization, right-sizing, FinOps practices, and cloud-native architecture adoption.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent cloud orchestration dynamically places workloads across providers based on real-time cost, performance, and compliance requirements.

items.text: Technical debt is identified, quantified, and systematically reduced through modernization efforts.

rubric.initial: Technical debt is untracked and growing. Legacy systems are a major constraint on agility.
rubric.basic: Some technical debt is recognized but there is no systematic approach to quantification or reduction.
rubric.intermediate: Technical debt is inventoried, risk-assessed, and addressed through dedicated modernization initiatives with allocated funding.
rubric.advanced: Automated technical debt detection integrates with planning processes, with TCO-based prioritization and modernization outcome tracking.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous modernization uses AI to identify refactoring opportunities, predict end-of-life impacts, and optimize the modernization portfolio.

items.text: Infrastructure reliability is engineered through redundancy, disaster recovery, and resilience testing.

rubric.initial: No redundancy or DR capabilities exist. Single points of failure are common and untested.
rubric.basic: Basic backups exist but disaster recovery plans are incomplete, untested, or outdated.
rubric.intermediate: Redundancy is implemented for critical systems with documented DR plans that are tested at least annually.
rubric.advanced: Active-active architectures, automated failover, and regular chaos engineering exercises ensure high resilience across all critical systems.
rubric.optimizing: Self-healing infrastructure with continuous resilience validation, automated chaos testing, and adaptive recovery mechanisms achieves near-zero downtime.

items.text: Infrastructure management includes comprehensive disaster recovery and business continuity capabilities.

rubric.initial: No disaster recovery or business continuity plans exist for IT infrastructure.
rubric.basic: Basic backup procedures exist but recovery capabilities are untested and incomplete.
rubric.intermediate: Documented DR/BC plans cover critical systems with regular testing and defined recovery objectives.
rubric.advanced: Automated failover and recovery processes meet aggressive RTOs and RPOs with continuous testing.
rubric.optimizing: Self-healing infrastructure with chaos engineering practices ensures continuous resilience improvement.

items.text: User research is conducted to understand needs, behaviors, and pain points before designing solutions.

rubric.initial: No user research is conducted. Solutions are designed based on assumptions and technical requirements only.
rubric.basic: Occasional user feedback is gathered but research is not systematic or integrated into the design process.
rubric.intermediate: Structured UX research methods (interviews, usability testing, surveys) are applied consistently for major initiatives.
rubric.advanced: Continuous user research programs combine qualitative and quantitative methods, with research repositories and insight synthesis.
rubric.optimizing: AI-augmented research provides real-time behavioral insights, predictive user modeling, and continuous validation of design hypotheses.

items.text: A design system ensures visual and interaction consistency across all IT solutions.

rubric.initial: No design system or UI standards exist. Every application looks and behaves differently.
rubric.basic: Basic style guides exist but are incomplete and inconsistently applied across teams.
rubric.intermediate: A comprehensive design system with reusable components, patterns, and documentation is maintained and adopted across teams.
rubric.advanced: The design system includes coded component libraries, automated compliance checking, and contribution processes from all teams.
rubric.optimizing: An intelligent design system adapts to user preferences, generates context-aware layouts, and evolves based on usage analytics.

items.text: Accessibility standards are implemented to ensure solutions are usable by people of all abilities.

rubric.initial: Accessibility is not considered in solution design. Many systems are inaccessible to users with disabilities.
rubric.basic: Some awareness of accessibility exists but compliance with standards (e.g., WCAG) is inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Accessibility standards are defined and integrated into design and development processes with regular auditing.
rubric.advanced: Automated accessibility testing is embedded in CI/CD pipelines, with user testing involving people with disabilities.
rubric.optimizing: AI-assisted accessibility ensures all solutions exceed standards, with adaptive interfaces that personalize to individual user needs.

items.text: User-centered design processes are embedded in the solution delivery lifecycle.

rubric.initial: Design is an afterthought. User interfaces are created by developers without design expertise.
