rubric.basic: Some projects involve designers, but UCD processes are not consistently applied across the organization.
rubric.intermediate: UCD processes are integrated into the SDLC with design reviews, prototyping, and iterative user testing for all projects.
rubric.advanced: Design thinking is a core competency with dedicated UX teams, executive sponsorship, and design quality metrics.
rubric.optimizing: Design excellence drives competitive advantage, with AI-assisted design generation, continuous experimentation, and personalization at scale.

items.text: User satisfaction and experience metrics are tracked and used to drive design improvements.

rubric.initial: No UX metrics are captured. User satisfaction is unknown.
rubric.basic: Occasional surveys capture user satisfaction but metrics are not systematically tracked or acted upon.
rubric.intermediate: UX metrics (SUS, NPS, task completion rates) are regularly measured and reported with improvement targets.
rubric.advanced: Real-time experience analytics combine behavioral data, session replay, and sentiment analysis to drive continuous UX optimization.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive UX analytics anticipate user frustration, recommend design changes, and A/B test improvements autonomously.

items.text: User experience metrics are collected and used to drive continuous improvement of IT solutions.

rubric.initial: No user experience metrics are collected for IT solutions.
rubric.basic: Occasional user feedback is gathered but not systematically measured or acted upon.
rubric.intermediate: UX metrics are regularly collected through analytics and surveys with documented improvement actions.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive UX measurement combines behavioral analytics, sentiment analysis, and task completion metrics.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time UX optimization uses A/B testing and AI-driven personalization to continuously improve experiences.

items.text: Training programs are developed and delivered to support the adoption of IT systems and tools.

rubric.initial: No user training is provided. Users are left to figure out new systems on their own.
rubric.basic: Basic training materials are created for major rollouts but quality and availability are inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Structured training programs with multiple delivery formats (classroom, e-learning, video) support all major IT deployments.
rubric.advanced: Role-based, just-in-time training is integrated into applications with learning management systems tracking completion and competency.
rubric.optimizing: AI-personalized learning paths adapt to individual skill levels and learning styles, with in-context guidance and microlearning.

items.text: Training effectiveness is measured and programs are refined based on learning outcomes.

rubric.initial: Training effectiveness is never measured. Impact on user productivity is unknown.
rubric.basic: Participant satisfaction surveys are conducted post-training but knowledge retention and behavioral change are not measured.
rubric.intermediate: Training effectiveness is assessed at multiple levels (reaction, learning, behavior) using the Kirkpatrick model or equivalent.
rubric.advanced: Learning analytics correlate training completion with productivity metrics, error rates, and support ticket volumes to demonstrate ROI.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive learning platforms use real-time performance data to continuously optimize training content, timing, and delivery methods.

items.text: Digital literacy programs ensure all users can effectively use core IT tools and platforms.

rubric.initial: No digital literacy programs exist. Wide variation in user competency hampers productivity.
rubric.basic: Basic digital literacy resources are available but participation is optional and uptake is low.
rubric.intermediate: Structured digital literacy programs with competency assessments address skill gaps across the user population.
rubric.advanced: Personalized digital literacy journeys are integrated with onboarding and role transitions, with competency-based progression.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven skill assessment continuously identifies literacy gaps and delivers targeted interventions to maintain high organizational digital fluency.

items.text: Change management and adoption support are provided for all significant IT deployments.

rubric.initial: No change management support is provided. New system rollouts face high resistance and low adoption.
rubric.basic: Basic communications accompany deployments but structured change management is not consistently applied.
rubric.intermediate: Formal change management frameworks (e.g., ADKAR, Kotter) are applied to all significant deployments with dedicated resources.
rubric.advanced: Change management is integrated into delivery methodologies with adoption metrics, resistance management, and reinforcement mechanisms.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive adoption analytics identify resistance patterns early, enabling targeted interventions that achieve rapid, sustained behavior change.

items.text: Post-deployment support channels help users resolve issues and build proficiency over time.

rubric.initial: No post-deployment support exists beyond the IT help desk. Users struggle with new systems after go-live.
rubric.basic: FAQ documents and basic support are available post-deployment but are insufficient for complex user needs.
rubric.intermediate: Multi-channel post-deployment support includes floor walkers, power users, knowledge bases, and dedicated support periods.
rubric.advanced: Intelligent support with chatbots, in-app guidance, and peer support communities provides continuous assistance tailored to user proficiency levels.
rubric.optimizing: Self-improving support systems learn from user interactions to proactively surface guidance, predict support needs, and eliminate recurring issues.

items.text: Training effectiveness is measured through behavioral change and business outcome impact.

rubric.initial: Training effectiveness is not measured beyond attendance tracking.
rubric.basic: Satisfaction surveys follow training but impact on behavior or outcomes is not assessed.
rubric.intermediate: Training evaluation includes knowledge retention tests and behavioral change assessments.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive evaluation measures training impact on performance metrics and business outcomes.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous learning analytics optimize training content and delivery based on outcome correlation data.

items.text: Expected benefits are systematically identified and documented for all significant IT investments.

rubric.initial: Benefits are not identified or documented for IT investments.
rubric.basic: High-level benefits are stated in business cases but lack specificity or measurability.
rubric.intermediate: Benefits are documented with clear definitions, owners, metrics, and target realization timelines.
rubric.advanced: Benefits mapping links investments to strategic objectives with dependency modeling and risk assessment.
rubric.optimizing: AI-assisted benefits discovery identifies emergent and cross-portfolio benefits opportunities automatically.

items.text: Benefits realization plans define how and when expected benefits will be achieved and measured.

rubric.initial: No benefits realization plans exist; expected outcomes are vaguely stated.
rubric.basic: Informal plans exist for some investments but lack rigor and accountability.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized realization plans specify milestones, metrics, accountable owners, and measurement methods.
rubric.advanced: Integrated realization plans are tracked in portfolio management tools with automated progress reporting.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive realization plans adjust dynamically based on delivery progress and changing business conditions.

items.text: Benefit owners are assigned and held accountable for realizing planned benefits.

rubric.initial: No benefit owners are assigned; accountability for value delivery is unclear.
rubric.basic: Benefit owners are nominally assigned but accountability mechanisms are weak.
rubric.intermediate: Benefit owners have documented responsibilities with regular review and reporting requirements.
rubric.advanced: Benefit ownership is embedded in performance management with incentives tied to realization outcomes.
rubric.optimizing: Distributed accountability networks ensure cross-functional ownership with collaborative realization tracking.

items.text: Both tangible and intangible benefits are captured in a balanced benefits framework.

rubric.initial: Only financial benefits are considered; intangible benefits are ignored.
rubric.basic: Intangible benefits are acknowledged but not measured or systematically tracked.
rubric.intermediate: A balanced framework captures financial, operational, strategic, and intangible benefits with proxy metrics.
rubric.advanced: Advanced valuation techniques quantify intangible benefits with validated measurement models.
rubric.optimizing: Comprehensive value models continuously refine intangible benefit valuations using organizational outcome data.

items.text: Benefits dependencies and prerequisites are mapped to ensure realization conditions are met.

rubric.initial: Benefits dependencies are not considered; realization is assumed to happen automatically.
rubric.basic: Some awareness of dependencies exists but they are not formally mapped or tracked.
rubric.intermediate: Benefits dependency maps identify prerequisites, enablers, and risks for each planned benefit.
rubric.advanced: Integrated dependency tracking links benefits to change activities, technology deliverables, and business readiness.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic dependency models adjust realization plans in real time as conditions and prerequisites evolve.

items.text: Realized benefits are tracked against planned targets with regular reporting to stakeholders.

rubric.initial: Benefits are not tracked post-implementation; no reporting on value delivery exists.
rubric.basic: Occasional benefit reviews occur for selected projects but tracking is inconsistent.
rubric.intermediate: Regular benefits tracking reports compare realized vs. planned benefits with variance explanations.
rubric.advanced: Real-time benefits dashboards provide portfolio-level visibility with drill-down to individual investments.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive benefits analytics forecast realization trajectories and recommend acceleration actions.

items.text: Lessons learned from benefits realization are captured and applied to improve future investments.

rubric.initial: No lessons learned process exists for benefits realization.
rubric.basic: Lessons are captured informally for some projects but rarely influence future investment decisions.
rubric.intermediate: A structured lessons learned process feeds insights into investment appraisal and benefits planning.
rubric.advanced: A benefits knowledge base provides searchable guidance with pattern analysis across the portfolio.
rubric.optimizing: Machine learning analyzes realization patterns to continuously improve benefits forecasting accuracy.

items.text: Benefits realization is integrated with organizational change management to drive adoption.

rubric.initial: Benefits realization and change management are completely disconnected activities.
rubric.basic: Some awareness exists that change management affects benefits but integration is minimal.
rubric.intermediate: Benefits realization plans explicitly include change management activities with coordinated timelines.
rubric.advanced: Integrated benefits and change programs use adoption metrics to predict and accelerate value delivery.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive change-benefit models dynamically adjust interventions based on real-time adoption and value signals.

items.text: Benefits realization is supported by robust data collection and measurement infrastructure.

rubric.initial: No data infrastructure exists to support benefits measurement.
rubric.basic: Some data is collected but measurement infrastructure is ad hoc and unreliable.
rubric.intermediate: Defined data collection processes and tools support consistent benefits measurement.
rubric.advanced: Automated data pipelines feed benefits dashboards with validated, real-time measurement data.
rubric.optimizing: Intelligent measurement infrastructure adapts data collection to evolving benefit definitions and metrics.

items.text: Dis-benefits and negative consequences of IT investments are identified and managed.

rubric.initial: Dis-benefits are never considered; only positive outcomes are planned for.
rubric.basic: Dis-benefits are occasionally acknowledged but not systematically identified or managed.
rubric.intermediate: Dis-benefit identification is part of the benefits planning process with documented mitigation strategies.
rubric.advanced: Active dis-benefit monitoring tracks negative consequences with trigger-based mitigation responses.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive models identify potential dis-benefits early and recommend preventive actions before they materialize.

items.text: A consistent value measurement framework is applied across all IT investments.

rubric.initial: No value measurement framework exists; value is not systematically assessed.
rubric.basic: Value is measured informally using different approaches across different investments.
rubric.intermediate: A standardized value measurement framework with defined metrics and methods is applied consistently.
rubric.advanced: The value framework integrates financial, operational, strategic, and stakeholder value dimensions.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive value measurement evolves continuously based on organizational learning and industry best practices.

items.text: Value governance ensures accountability for value delivery at portfolio, program, and project levels.

rubric.initial: No value governance exists; accountability for value delivery is absent.
rubric.basic: Value is discussed at governance meetings but without formal accountability or tracking.
rubric.intermediate: Value governance structures define roles, review cadences, and escalation paths for value delivery issues.
rubric.advanced: Multi-level value governance connects project-level delivery to portfolio-level strategic value realization.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive governance dynamically adjusts oversight intensity based on value delivery risk and performance signals.

items.text: Value delivery performance is benchmarked against industry standards and peer organizations.

rubric.initial: No benchmarking of value delivery performance is conducted.
rubric.basic: Informal comparisons with industry peers occur occasionally but lack rigor.
rubric.intermediate: Structured benchmarking compares value delivery performance against recognized frameworks and peer data.
rubric.advanced: Continuous benchmarking uses multiple data sources with normalization for organizational context.
rubric.optimizing: Automated benchmarking platforms provide real-time peer comparisons with actionable improvement recommendations.

items.text: Stakeholder satisfaction with IT value delivery is measured and acted upon.

rubric.initial: Stakeholder satisfaction with IT value is not measured.
rubric.basic: Occasional surveys capture satisfaction but results are not systematically acted upon.
rubric.intermediate: Regular satisfaction assessments drive documented improvement actions with follow-up tracking.
rubric.advanced: Multi-channel satisfaction measurement provides real-time feedback with automated response workflows.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive satisfaction models anticipate stakeholder concerns and trigger proactive engagement.

items.text: Benefits realization is integrated with portfolio management for enterprise-level value tracking.

rubric.initial: Benefits realization and portfolio management are separate, unconnected activities.
rubric.basic: Some benefits data is shared with portfolio managers but integration is informal.
rubric.intermediate: Benefits realization feeds portfolio reviews with structured value delivery reporting.
rubric.advanced: Integrated benefits-portfolio management provides enterprise value visibility with strategic alignment analysis.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time value streams connect project delivery to strategic outcomes across the enterprise portfolio.

items.text: A value-driven culture ensures IT initiatives are consistently evaluated for business impact.

rubric.initial: No value culture exists; IT initiatives proceed without business impact consideration.
rubric.basic: Value awareness exists in pockets but is not part of organizational culture.
rubric.intermediate: Value-driven practices are embedded in IT processes with regular communication of value principles.
rubric.advanced: A strong value culture drives decision-making at all levels with visible leadership commitment.
rubric.optimizing: Self-reinforcing value culture continuously evolves with organizational learning and external benchmarking.

items.text: Value realization capabilities are developed as a core organizational competency.

rubric.initial: No value realization competency development exists.
rubric.basic: Ad hoc training on benefits management is available to some staff.
rubric.intermediate: Structured training programs build value realization skills across IT and business teams.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive competency frameworks define and develop value realization capabilities at all levels.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous capability development adapts to emerging value management practices and organizational needs.

items.text: A formal portfolio governance framework guides IT investment selection and oversight.

rubric.initial: No portfolio governance exists; projects are approved individually without portfolio-level oversight.
rubric.basic: Basic governance exists but portfolio decisions are made inconsistently without formal criteria.
rubric.intermediate: A documented governance framework with defined roles, decision criteria, and review cadences is in place.
rubric.advanced: Portfolio governance integrates strategic planning, risk management, and value tracking in a unified process.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive governance models adjust oversight intensity based on portfolio risk and strategic alignment signals.

items.text: Portfolio prioritization balances strategic alignment, risk, resource constraints, and expected value.

rubric.initial: Projects are prioritized based on urgency or executive preference without systematic evaluation.
rubric.basic: A basic prioritization list exists but does not consistently weigh multiple decision criteria.
rubric.intermediate: Multi-criteria prioritization models balance strategic fit, risk, resources, and value with documented trade-offs.
rubric.advanced: Optimization algorithms identify the highest-value portfolio mix given constraints and dependencies.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time portfolio optimization continuously rebalances investments based on evolving conditions and value delivery.

items.text: Portfolio health is monitored through integrated dashboards covering schedule, budget, risk, and value.

rubric.initial: No portfolio-level health monitoring exists; project status is tracked in isolation if at all.
rubric.basic: Periodic portfolio status reports are compiled manually from individual project updates.
rubric.intermediate: Integrated dashboards aggregate project data into portfolio views with health indicators and trend analysis.
rubric.advanced: Real-time portfolio dashboards provide predictive health analytics with automated early warning systems.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven portfolio intelligence anticipates issues, recommends interventions, and simulates decision impacts.

items.text: Portfolio reviews regularly assess whether the investment mix supports strategic objectives.

rubric.initial: No portfolio reviews are conducted; the investment mix evolves organically without strategic assessment.
rubric.basic: Annual portfolio reviews occur but rarely result in significant rebalancing or project termination.
rubric.intermediate: Quarterly portfolio reviews evaluate strategic alignment and trigger rebalancing decisions with documented rationale.
rubric.advanced: Continuous portfolio assessment integrates with strategy reviews, enabling rapid pivots in investment direction.
rubric.optimizing: Portfolio reviews are augmented by scenario simulation and strategic impact modeling for optimal decision-making.

items.text: Portfolio risk is managed holistically considering aggregate and correlated risks across projects.

rubric.initial: Portfolio-level risk is not assessed; risk management occurs only at the project level.
rubric.basic: Major portfolio risks are known but aggregate risk exposure is not quantified.
rubric.intermediate: Portfolio risk assessments aggregate project risks and identify correlated risk exposures.
rubric.advanced: Quantitative portfolio risk models assess aggregate exposure with Monte Carlo simulation and stress testing.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time portfolio risk engines continuously model aggregate exposure and recommend mitigation strategies.

items.text: Resource capacity and demand are managed at the portfolio level to optimize allocation.

rubric.initial: Resource allocation is done project-by-project with no portfolio-level visibility into capacity.
rubric.basic: Some visibility into resource demand exists but allocation conflicts are resolved reactively.
rubric.intermediate: Portfolio-level resource management balances demand against capacity with documented allocation decisions.
rubric.advanced: Predictive resource planning models optimize allocation across the portfolio with scenario analysis.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven resource optimization dynamically reallocates capacity based on priority shifts and delivery signals.

items.text: Inter-project dependencies are identified and managed to reduce portfolio-level risk.

rubric.initial: Dependencies between projects are not identified or managed at the portfolio level.
rubric.basic: Major dependencies are known informally but not systematically tracked or managed.
rubric.intermediate: A dependency register is maintained with regular reviews and impact assessments for key dependencies.
rubric.advanced: Dependency mapping tools visualize and analyze cross-project impacts with automated alert systems.
rubric.optimizing: Network analysis algorithms optimize project sequencing and identify critical path risks across the portfolio.

items.text: Portfolio delivery performance is measured and continuously improved using outcome data.

rubric.initial: Portfolio delivery performance is not measured; success is anecdotal.
rubric.basic: Basic delivery metrics (on-time, on-budget) are tracked but not used for systematic improvement.
rubric.intermediate: Comprehensive delivery metrics are analyzed to identify patterns and drive improvement initiatives.
rubric.advanced: Delivery performance baselines enable predictive estimation and targeted capability building.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous delivery optimization uses machine learning to improve estimation accuracy and execution efficiency.

items.text: Portfolio composition is explicitly linked to strategic objectives with measurable contribution targets.

rubric.initial: No explicit linkage exists between the project portfolio and strategic objectives.
rubric.basic: Projects are loosely tagged to strategic themes but contribution is not measured.
rubric.intermediate: Each project maps to strategic objectives with defined contribution metrics and targets.
rubric.advanced: Portfolio-level strategic contribution dashboards show aggregate progress toward each objective.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic strategy mapping continuously adjusts portfolio priorities based on strategic progress signals.

items.text: Portfolio value delivery is tracked using leading indicators that predict future value realization.

rubric.initial: No value tracking exists at the portfolio level.
rubric.basic: Lagging financial indicators are reported but leading indicators are not used.
rubric.intermediate: A balanced set of leading and lagging indicators tracks portfolio value delivery.
rubric.advanced: Predictive value models use leading indicators to forecast portfolio-level value realization.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven value prediction continuously refines forecasts and recommends portfolio optimization actions.

items.text: Innovation investments are managed as a distinct portfolio segment with appropriate governance.

rubric.initial: Innovation investments are not distinguished from other IT investments.
rubric.basic: Some innovation projects exist but are governed using the same criteria as operational investments.
rubric.intermediate: An innovation portfolio segment has tailored governance, metrics, and risk tolerance appropriate to innovation.
rubric.advanced: Innovation portfolio management uses stage-gate models with pivot/persevere decisions based on learning milestones.
rubric.optimizing: Adaptive innovation governance balances exploration and exploitation using real-time market and technology signals.

items.text: Portfolio decisions consider organizational capacity for change to avoid change saturation.

rubric.initial: Change capacity is not considered in portfolio decisions; change saturation is a frequent problem.
rubric.basic: Awareness of change fatigue exists but it does not systematically influence portfolio decisions.
rubric.intermediate: Change impact assessments are included in portfolio planning with documented capacity thresholds.
rubric.advanced: Change capacity models quantify organizational absorption limits and constrain portfolio commitments.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic change capacity sensing adjusts portfolio pacing in real time based on adoption and engagement data.

items.text: Portfolio management practices are benchmarked and continuously improved using maturity models.

rubric.initial: Portfolio management maturity is not assessed or benchmarked.
rubric.basic: Awareness of portfolio management best practices exists but no formal benchmarking occurs.
rubric.intermediate: Regular maturity assessments benchmark portfolio practices against recognized standards.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive maturity improvement programs drive systematic enhancement of portfolio management capabilities.
rubric.optimizing: Continuous maturity monitoring with automated benchmarking drives adaptive improvement of portfolio practices.

items.text: Portfolio management practices incorporate lean and agile principles for faster value delivery.

rubric.initial: Portfolio management uses only traditional waterfall approaches.
rubric.basic: Some agile projects exist within the portfolio but governance remains traditional.
rubric.intermediate: Lean portfolio management practices enable iterative funding, adaptive planning, and incremental delivery.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive lean-agile portfolio management aligns strategy, funding, and delivery with value streams.
rubric.optimizing: Self-organizing portfolio ecosystems dynamically balance exploration and exploitation for maximum value.

items.text: Portfolio management tools provide integrated visibility across traditional and agile delivery methods.

rubric.initial: No integrated portfolio tools exist; tracking is fragmented across multiple systems.
rubric.basic: Basic portfolio tracking tools exist but do not accommodate different delivery methods.
rubric.intermediate: Integrated tools provide portfolio visibility across waterfall, agile, and hybrid delivery approaches.
rubric.advanced: Advanced portfolio platforms offer real-time analytics, scenario modeling, and cross-methodology reporting.
rubric.optimizing: AI-augmented portfolio platforms provide predictive insights and optimization recommendations across all delivery methods.

items.text: TCO models capture the full lifecycle costs of IT assets and services including hidden costs.

rubric.initial: TCO is not calculated; only acquisition costs are considered in IT decisions.
rubric.basic: Basic TCO estimates exist for some assets but omit significant cost categories like support and disposal.
rubric.intermediate: Standardized TCO models capture acquisition, operation, maintenance, and disposal costs for all major assets.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive TCO models include indirect costs, opportunity costs, and risk-adjusted projections.
rubric.optimizing: Dynamic TCO models update continuously with actual cost data and predict future lifecycle costs with high accuracy.

items.text: TCO analysis informs technology selection and architecture decisions.

rubric.initial: Technology decisions are made without TCO consideration; initial cost is the primary factor.
rubric.basic: TCO is occasionally referenced in major technology decisions but analysis lacks rigor.
rubric.intermediate: TCO analysis is a required input to technology selection with standardized comparison methodologies.
rubric.advanced: TCO models integrate with enterprise architecture tools to evaluate architectural alternatives holistically.
rubric.optimizing: Automated TCO simulation evaluates technology options against multiple scenarios and strategic criteria simultaneously.

items.text: Lifecycle cost projections are maintained for the IT asset portfolio to support financial planning.

rubric.initial: No lifecycle cost projections exist for IT assets.
rubric.basic: Lifecycle costs are estimated for some assets but projections are not maintained over time.
rubric.intermediate: Portfolio-level lifecycle cost projections are updated annually and integrated with budget planning.
rubric.advanced: Continuous lifecycle cost tracking enables proactive refresh planning and financial forecasting.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive lifecycle models anticipate cost inflection points and automatically trigger planning workflows.

items.text: Cloud vs. on-premises TCO comparisons use consistent methodologies for informed sourcing decisions.

rubric.initial: No TCO comparison is performed between cloud and on-premises options.
rubric.basic: Ad hoc comparisons exist but use inconsistent assumptions and incomplete cost categories.
rubric.intermediate: A standardized methodology compares cloud and on-premises TCO including migration and operational costs.
rubric.advanced: Dynamic comparison models account for workload variability, scaling patterns, and multi-cloud scenarios.
rubric.optimizing: Automated TCO engines continuously evaluate optimal placement across hybrid/multi-cloud environments.

items.text: TCO models account for environmental and sustainability costs including carbon impact.

rubric.initial: Environmental and sustainability costs are not included in TCO calculations.
rubric.basic: Environmental costs are acknowledged but not quantified in TCO models.
rubric.intermediate: TCO models include quantified environmental costs such as energy consumption and carbon offsets.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive sustainability-adjusted TCO models inform technology decisions with full environmental costing.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time sustainability TCO tracking drives continuous reduction in environmental impact alongside financial optimization.

items.text: Systematic cost optimization identifies and eliminates waste across the IT portfolio.

rubric.initial: No systematic effort to identify or eliminate IT cost waste exists.
rubric.basic: Periodic cost-cutting exercises occur but are reactive and not based on systematic analysis.
rubric.intermediate: Regular cost optimization reviews use TCO data to identify waste and prioritize reduction initiatives.
rubric.advanced: Continuous optimization programs track savings targets with automated waste detection across the portfolio.
rubric.optimizing: AI-driven optimization continuously identifies savings opportunities and models the impact of cost changes on value.

items.text: Technology rationalization reduces TCO by consolidating redundant systems and licenses.

rubric.initial: No technology rationalization effort exists; redundant systems and licenses proliferate.
rubric.basic: Occasional rationalization projects address obvious duplication but lack a systematic approach.
rubric.intermediate: A technology rationalization program uses application portfolio data to prioritize consolidation efforts.
rubric.advanced: Continuous rationalization integrates with architecture governance to prevent redundancy and optimize licensing.
rubric.optimizing: Automated discovery and analysis tools continuously identify rationalization opportunities with business impact modeling.

items.text: Cost optimization initiatives are evaluated for their impact on service quality and business value.

rubric.initial: Cost cuts are made without assessing impact on service quality or business value.
rubric.basic: Service impact is informally considered but not systematically evaluated for cost reduction decisions.
rubric.intermediate: Cost optimization business cases include documented impact assessments on service levels and business value.
rubric.advanced: Value-adjusted cost optimization balances savings against service quality and strategic capability impacts.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time value-cost modeling enables continuous optimization while maintaining or improving service delivery outcomes.

items.text: TCO analysis is a mandatory input to all significant IT investment and procurement decisions.

rubric.initial: TCO analysis is not required for IT decisions; acquisition cost dominates.
rubric.basic: TCO is occasionally referenced but is not a mandatory decision input.
rubric.intermediate: Policy mandates TCO analysis for decisions above defined thresholds with standardized templates.
rubric.advanced: Automated TCO generation is integrated into procurement and investment workflows as a required gate.
rubric.optimizing: Real-time TCO insights are embedded in all decision points with context-aware recommendations.

items.text: TCO data quality is managed to ensure accuracy and reliability of lifecycle cost information.

rubric.initial: TCO data is unreliable or nonexistent with no quality management.
rubric.basic: Some TCO data exists but accuracy varies and there is no quality assurance process.
rubric.intermediate: TCO data quality standards are defined with regular validation and correction processes.
rubric.advanced: Automated data quality monitoring ensures TCO accuracy with anomaly detection and correction workflows.
rubric.optimizing: Self-healing TCO data systems continuously validate and improve data accuracy using multiple source reconciliation.

items.text: TCO trends are analyzed to identify systemic cost drivers and inform strategic cost management.

rubric.initial: No trend analysis of TCO data is performed.
rubric.basic: Occasional ad hoc analysis of cost trends occurs but findings are not actionable.
rubric.intermediate: Regular TCO trend analysis identifies cost drivers and informs targeted cost management initiatives.
rubric.advanced: Advanced analytics reveal hidden cost patterns and predict future TCO trajectories for strategic planning.
rubric.optimizing: Predictive TCO models continuously identify emerging cost drivers and recommend proactive interventions.

items.text: TCO knowledge is shared across the organization to build cost-awareness and improve decision-making.

rubric.initial: TCO concepts and data are confined to a small group with no organizational sharing.
rubric.basic: TCO information is shared on request but there is no proactive knowledge dissemination.
rubric.intermediate: TCO training and reference materials are available with regular communication of key cost insights.
rubric.advanced: TCO dashboards and decision support tools are accessible to all relevant decision-makers.
rubric.optimizing: An organizational TCO culture embeds lifecycle cost thinking into all technology decisions at every level.

items.text: TCO models are validated against actual costs to continuously improve estimation accuracy.

rubric.initial: TCO estimates are never compared to actual costs; accuracy is unknown.
rubric.basic: Occasional post-hoc comparisons occur but findings do not improve future estimates.
rubric.intermediate: Systematic comparison of estimated vs. actual TCO drives calibration of estimation models.
rubric.advanced: Continuous TCO model validation uses machine learning to improve estimation accuracy over time.
rubric.optimizing: Self-calibrating TCO models achieve high accuracy through continuous learning from outcome data across the portfolio.

items.text: TCO insights are communicated to decision-makers in formats that drive informed technology choices.

rubric.initial: TCO data is not communicated to decision-makers.
rubric.basic: Raw TCO data is occasionally shared but not in actionable formats.
rubric.intermediate: TCO summaries are tailored for different audiences with clear implications for technology decisions.
rubric.advanced: Interactive TCO tools enable decision-makers to explore scenarios and compare options independently.
rubric.optimizing: Personalized TCO insights are proactively delivered to decision-makers at the point of decision.

items.text: TCO awareness programs build organizational capability in lifecycle cost thinking.

rubric.initial: No TCO awareness programs exist; lifecycle costing is understood by very few.
rubric.basic: Occasional presentations introduce TCO concepts but sustained awareness building is absent.
rubric.intermediate: Structured TCO awareness programs reach all relevant decision-makers with practical tools and examples.
rubric.advanced: Comprehensive TCO education is embedded in procurement, architecture, and project management training.
rubric.optimizing: A TCO-aware culture drives lifecycle cost consideration in all technology decisions at every organizational level.
